toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate per-repo team writeup files from skill_validation_*.md data."""

import re
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

DATA_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "data"
REPOS_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "repos"
OUTPUT_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "writeups"

TOTAL_REPOS = 42  # total repos reviewed

TEMPLATE_INTRO = """\
# Bug Findings: {repo_name}

## Background

I've been doing some experimentation into building a skill that finds bugs in charms, based on all the bugs that have already been fixed. You don't need to read all the gory details, but they are in the [Charming with Claude](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude) experiment "2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data" if you do.

After validating the skill against repos not used in its construction, I ran it across {total_repos} repositories. The findings below are the results for **{repo_name}**. Each finding includes the location, a description of the issue, its impact, the relevant code, and a suggested fix.

I have not verified these findings against the running charm — they are based on static analysis of the source code at the time of review. Some may be mitigated by runtime conditions not visible in the code, or may have already been addressed.

I don't want to open a bunch of issues or PRs in your repo with what might turn out to be AI slop. On the other hand, if there are actual bugs here, it would be good to solve them. Also, somewhat selfishly, I would love extra data on any false positives to feed back into the skill, to try to make it something actually useful across charming.

Basically: the ball is in your court. You can ignore these, you can review them and open issues yourself (in many cases Copilot should be able to provide a draft fix; these aren't complex issues). You can ask me to open issues and/or PRs.

(If there's anything that looks to me (not just AI) like a security finding, they were excluded and I'll handle them separately.)

## Summary

- **Findings**: {total} ({severity_summary})
- **Code areas reviewed**: {areas}

Note that the severity and area were picked by AI and I don't necessarily agree with them all, but they're a handy base classification.

## Findings
"""

SEVERITY_ORDER = ["Critical", "High", "Medium", "Low"]

PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_NONE = (
    "\n\n> **Note**: `None` values in Pebble environment dicts do cause `add_layer` "
    "to fail. However, non-string values like `int`, `bool`, and `float` are "
    "silently coerced to strings by Pebble's Go YAML decoder (see "
    "[pebble-non-string-env.md](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude"
    "/blob/main/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis"
    "/pebble-non-string-env.md) for details). The `None` part of this finding is "
    "a real crash; the non-string part is a code quality issue with no runtime impact.\n"
)

PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_NON_STRING_ONLY = (
    "\n\n> **Note**: Pebble silently coerces non-string scalar values (`int`, `bool`, "
    "`float`) to strings via its Go YAML decoder — so this does not crash at runtime "
    "(see [pebble-non-string-env.md](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude"
    "/blob/main/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis"
    "/pebble-non-string-env.md) for details). "
    "It is still technically incorrect and relies on undocumented behaviour, but "
    "the practical impact is minimal.\n"
)

PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_BYTES = (
    "\n\n> **Note**: Non-string scalar values (`int`, `bool`, `float`) in Pebble "
    "environment dicts are silently coerced to strings by Pebble's Go YAML decoder "
    "(see [pebble-non-string-env.md](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude"
    "/blob/main/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis"
    "/pebble-non-string-env.md) for details). "
    "However, `bytes` objects may not be handled the same way.\n"
)


def get_github_url(repo_dir_name: str) -> str | None:
    """Get the GitHub base URL for a repo."""
    repo_path = REPOS_DIR / repo_dir_name
    if not repo_path.exists():
        return None
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["git", "-C", str(repo_path), "remote", "get-url", "origin"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
        )
        url = result.stdout.strip().removesuffix(".git")
        # Get the default branch
        result2 = subprocess.run(
            ["git", "-C", str(repo_path), "rev-parse", "--abbrev-ref", "HEAD"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
        )
        branch = result2.stdout.strip()
        return f"{url}/blob/{branch}"
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError:
        return None


def get_repo_dir_name(repo_name: str) -> str | None:
    """Map the repo name from the validation file to the directory name in repos/."""
    # Direct match
    if (REPOS_DIR / repo_name).exists():
        return repo_name

    # Try common variations
    variations = [
        repo_name,
        f"{repo_name}-operator",
        f"{repo_name}-k8s-operator",
    ]
    for v in variations:
        if (REPOS_DIR / v).exists():
            return v

    # Known mappings for tricky names
    known = {
        "grafana-agent": "grafana-agent-k8s-operator",
        "seldon-core": "seldon-core-operator",
        "zookeeper": "zookeeper-k8s-operator",
        "sdcore-amf-k8s-operator": "sdcore-amf-operator",
    }
    if repo_name in known and (REPOS_DIR / known[repo_name]).exists():
        return known[repo_name]

    # Fuzzy: check all repo dirs for a substring match
    core = repo_name.replace("-operator", "").replace("-k8s", "")
    for d in sorted(REPOS_DIR.iterdir()):
        if d.is_dir() and core in d.name:
            return d.name

    return None


def parse_validation_file(filepath: Path) -> dict:
    """Parse a skill_validation_*.md file into structured data."""
    text = filepath.read_text()

    # Extract repo name from header
    header_match = re.match(r"## Bug Review:\s*(.+)", text)
    repo_name = header_match.group(1).strip() if header_match else filepath.stem

    # Clean up repo name (remove " src/ directory", " src/", trailing slashes, parentheticals, etc.)
    repo_name = re.sub(r"\s*(src/?|src/ directory|--.*|\(.*\))", "", repo_name).strip()
    repo_name = repo_name.rstrip("/").strip()
    # Special cases
    repo_name = repo_name.replace("directory", "").strip()

    # Extract summary line
    findings_match = re.search(
        r"\*\*Findings\*\*:\s*(\d+)\s*\(([^)]+)\)", text
    )
    total = int(findings_match.group(1)) if findings_match else 0
    severity_summary = findings_match.group(2) if findings_match else ""

    # Extract areas
    areas_match = re.search(
        r"\*\*Code areas reviewed\*\*:\s*(.+)", text
    )
    areas = areas_match.group(1).strip() if areas_match else ""

    # Split into findings section and confirmed safe section
    findings_text = text
    confirmed_safe_idx = text.find("### Confirmed Safe")
    if confirmed_safe_idx >= 0:
        findings_text = text[:confirmed_safe_idx]

    # Parse individual findings
    finding_pattern = re.compile(
        r"####\s+\[([^\]]+)\]\s+(.+?)\s*\((\w+)\)\s*\n(.*?)(?=####|\Z)",
        re.DOTALL,
    )

    findings = []
    for match in finding_pattern.finditer(findings_text):
        bug_id = match.group(1)
        title_raw = match.group(2).strip()
        severity = match.group(3)
        body = match.group(4).strip()

        # Determine if this is a security finding
        is_security = "Security --" in title_raw or "Security —" in title_raw

        # Clean up title: remove category prefix like "Action Handler -- "
        title = re.sub(r"^.*?--\s*", "", title_raw).strip()
        # Get category
        category_match = re.match(r"^(.*?)\s*--", title_raw)
        category = category_match.group(1).strip() if category_match else ""

        findings.append({
            "bug_id": bug_id,
            "title": title,
            "category": category,
            "severity": severity,
            "body": body,
            "is_security": is_security,
        })

    return {
        "repo_name": repo_name,
        "total": total,
        "severity_summary": severity_summary,
        "areas": areas,
        "findings": findings,
    }


def make_github_link(location: str, github_base: str | None) -> str:
    """Convert a location like `src/charm.py:358` to a GitHub link."""
    if not github_base:
        return f"`{location}`"

    # Parse file:line
    loc_match = re.match(r"`?([^`:]+?)(?::(\d+))?`?$", location.strip())
    if not loc_match:
        return f"`{location}`"

    filepath = loc_match.group(1)
    line = loc_match.group(2)

    if line:
        # Could be multiple lines like "425,449,474,498"
        lines = line.split(",")
        first_line = lines[0].strip()
        return f"[`{filepath}:{line}`]({github_base}/{filepath}#L{first_line})"
    else:
        return f"[`{filepath}`]({github_base}/{filepath})"


def transform_finding_body(body: str, github_base: str | None) -> str:
    """Transform a finding body from internal format to writeup format."""
    lines = body.split("\n")
    output_lines = []
    skip_until_next_field = False

    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        line = lines[i]

        # Skip Pattern line
        if line.startswith("- **Pattern**:"):
            i += 1
            continue

        # Skip Historical precedent
        if line.startswith("- **Historical precedent**:"):
            i += 1
            continue

        # Transform Location to include GitHub link
        loc_match = re.match(r"- \*\*Location\*\*:\s*(.+)", line)
        if loc_match:
            location = loc_match.group(1).strip()
            # Handle multiple locations (e.g., "src/charm.py:425,449,474,498")
            linked = make_github_link(location, github_base)
            output_lines.append(f"**Location**: {linked}")
            output_lines.append("")
            i += 1
            continue

        # Transform field markers from "- **X**:" to "**X**:"
        field_match = re.match(r"- \*\*(\w[\w\s]*)\*\*:\s*(.*)", line)
        if field_match:
            field_name = field_match.group(1)
            field_value = field_match.group(2)

            # Rename Evidence -> Code
            if field_name == "Evidence":
                field_name = "Code"

            # Ensure blank line before each field for readability
            if output_lines and output_lines[-1] != "":
                output_lines.append("")
            output_lines.append(f"**{field_name}**: {field_value}")
            i += 1
            continue

        # Pass through everything else (code blocks, etc.)
        output_lines.append(line)
        i += 1

    # Clean up trailing whitespace and excessive blank lines
    result = "\n".join(output_lines).strip()
    result = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", result)
    return result


def generate_writeup(data: dict, github_base: str | None) -> str:
    """Generate the full writeup markdown for a repo."""
    # Filter out security findings
    non_security = [f for f in data["findings"] if not f["is_security"]]

    if not non_security:
        return ""

    # Recalculate counts
    severity_counts = {s: 0 for s in SEVERITY_ORDER}
    for f in non_security:
        if f["severity"] in severity_counts:
            severity_counts[f["severity"]] += 1

    total = sum(severity_counts.values())
    severity_parts = []
    for s in SEVERITY_ORDER:
        if severity_counts[s] > 0:
            severity_parts.append(f"{severity_counts[s]} {s}")
    severity_summary = ", ".join(severity_parts)

    output = TEMPLATE_INTRO.format(
        repo_name=data["repo_name"],
        total_repos=TOTAL_REPOS,
        total=total,
        severity_summary=severity_summary,
        areas=data["areas"],
    )

    # Group findings by severity
    for severity in SEVERITY_ORDER:
        findings_at_level = [
            f for f in non_security if f["severity"] == severity
        ]
        if not findings_at_level:
            continue

        output += f"\n### {severity}\n\n"

        for finding in findings_at_level:
            output += f"#### {finding['bug_id']}: {finding['title']}\n\n"
            transformed = transform_finding_body(finding["body"], github_base)
            output += transformed
            # Add Pebble env caveat if applicable
            caveat = get_pebble_env_caveat(finding)
            if caveat:
                output += caveat
            output += "\n\n---\n\n"

    return output.rstrip("\n-— \n") + "\n"


def main():
    OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

    validation_files = sorted(DATA_DIR.glob("skill_validation_*.md"))
    print(f"Found {len(validation_files)} validation files")

    generated = 0
    skipped_no_findings = 0
    skipped_all_security = 0

    for vf in validation_files:
        data = parse_validation_file(vf)

        # Find the repo directory
        repo_dir = get_repo_dir_name(data["repo_name"])
        github_base = get_github_url(repo_dir) if repo_dir else None

        if not github_base:
            print(f"  WARNING: No GitHub URL for {data['repo_name']} (dir: {repo_dir})")

        non_security = [f for f in data["findings"] if not f["is_security"]]
        if not non_security:
            if data["findings"]:
                skipped_all_security += 1
                print(f"  SKIP (all security): {data['repo_name']}")
            else:
                skipped_no_findings += 1
                print(f"  SKIP (no findings): {data['repo_name']}")
            continue

        writeup = generate_writeup(data, github_base)
        if not writeup:
            skipped_no_findings += 1
            continue

        # Output filename: use the repo name, sanitised
        safe_name = re.sub(r"[^a-zA-Z0-9_-]", "_", data["repo_name"])
        output_path = OUTPUT_DIR / f"{safe_name}.md"
        output_path.write_text(writeup)
        generated += 1

        # Count findings
        sec_count = len(data["findings"]) - len(non_security)
        sec_note = f" ({sec_count} security excluded)" if sec_count else ""
        print(f"  OK: {data['repo_name']} -> {output_path.name} ({len(non_security)} findings{sec_note})")

    print(f"\nDone: {generated} writeups generated, {skipped_no_findings} skipped (no findings), {skipped_all_security} skipped (all security)")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_opensearch.md:42:- **Issue**: The environment variable `SNAP_LOG_DIR` is set to `f"${self._SNAP_COMMON}/ops/snap/logs"`. Because `$` is a literal character in Python f-strings (only `{...}` triggers interpolation), the `$` is preserved verbatim. The resulting value is `$/var/snap/opensearch/common/ops/snap/logs` -- a path starting with a literal `$` character. The other env vars on adjacent lines correctly use `f"{self._SNAP}/..."` and `f"{self.paths.home}/..."` without the `$`.
/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-43-- **Impact**: Any code or process that reads `SNAP_LOG_DIR` gets an invalid path starting with `$`. If used for log file creation, logs would be written to a non-existent directory (or a directory literally named `$var`), causing silent log loss or errors.

/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_sdcore_nms.md:32:- **Issue**: `JSON_HEADER` is a module-level mutable dictionary. In `_make_request`, `headers = JSON_HEADER` assigns a reference (not a copy), and then `headers["Authorization"] = f"Bearer {token}"` mutates the global dict. After the first authenticated request, the `Authorization` header persists in the global dict and is sent with all subsequent requests, including unauthenticated ones like `get_status()`.
/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_sdcore_nms.md-33-- **Impact**: Unauthenticated endpoints receive a stale `Authorization` header. If the token expires or becomes invalid, even status checks that should not require authentication could fail or behave unexpectedly. Within a single hook invocation, the first authenticated call pollutes all subsequent calls.

This `None` is then placed into the Pebble environment dict as `NGINX_BACKEND_SITE_NAME` and also interpolated into the nginx config template via `.format(**env_config)`, producing the literal string `"None"` in the generated nginx configuration.
/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_content_cache.md-69-- **Impact**: Nginx receives a configuration with `None` as the backend site name, which causes either a config parse error or proxying to a nonsensical hostname. The `.format()` call at line 457 will render it as the string `"None"` in the nginx config file, and Pebble will reject a `None` environment value.

/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_wordpress.md:36:- **Issue**: The database password from the MySQL relation is interpolated directly into a PHP `define()` statement using an f-string without escaping single quotes. If the password contains a single quote character (`'`), the generated `wp-config.php` will have a PHP syntax error, preventing WordPress from starting. In a worst-case scenario, a password containing `'); <PHP code> //` would result in arbitrary PHP code execution.
/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_wordpress.md-37-- **Impact**: A database password containing single quotes breaks WordPress entirely. While the MySQL charm typically generates safe passwords, any change in the database provider's password generation logic (or manual password setting) could trigger this. The same pattern applies to `DB_HOST`, `DB_NAME`, and `DB_USER` (lines 448-451), and to the WordPress secret keys (line 460).

If no such line exists (e.g., a certificate block lacks a friendlyName attribute, which can happen with certain PKCS12 formats or malformed stores), `alias_line` is `None`, and the subsequent `alias_line.split("friendlyName:", 1)` raises `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'split'`.

This function is called by `read_ca()` (used extensively in TLS certificate management) and by the snapshots manager. Any corruption or unexpected format in the PKCS12 store would crash the charm on every event until the store is manually repaired.

/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-42-- **Issue**: The environment variable `SNAP_LOG_DIR` is set to `f"${self._SNAP_COMMON}/ops/snap/logs"`. Because `$` is a literal character in Python f-strings (only `{...}` triggers interpolation), the `$` is preserved verbatim. The resulting value is `$/var/snap/opensearch/common/ops/snap/logs` -- a path starting with a literal `$` character. The other env vars on adjacent lines correctly use `f"{self._SNAP}/..."` and `f"{self.paths.home}/..."` without the `$`.
/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-43-- **Impact**: Any code or process that reads `SNAP_LOG_DIR` gets an invalid path starting with `$`. If used for log file creation, logs would be written to a non-existent directory (or a directory literally named `$var`), causing silent log loss or errors.

/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-42-- **Issue**: The environment variable `SNAP_LOG_DIR` is set to `f"${self._SNAP_COMMON}/ops/snap/logs"`. Because `$` is a literal character in Python f-strings (only `{...}` triggers interpolation), the `$` is preserved verbatim. The resulting value is `$/var/snap/opensearch/common/ops/snap/logs` -- a path starting with a literal `$` character. The other env vars on adjacent lines correctly use `f"{self._SNAP}/..."` and `f"{self.paths.home}/..."` without the `$`.
/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-43-- **Impact**: Any code or process that reads `SNAP_LOG_DIR` gets an invalid path starting with `$`. If used for log file creation, logs would be written to a non-existent directory (or a directory literally named `$var`), causing silent log loss or errors.

1→## Bug Review: cos-configuration-k8s-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 3 (0 Critical, 1 High, 2 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, configuration handling, status management, relation data, error handling, actions, URL/network, security
     6→- **Charm name**: cos-configuration-k8s
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Configuration -- Missing f-string prefix in proxy error logging (Medium)
    11→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:540` and `src/charm.py:547`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-002 (Missing f-string prefix)
    13→- **Issue**: Two `logger.info()` calls are missing the `f` prefix, so they log the literal strings `{proxy_host}` and `{proxy_port}` instead of the actual values.
    14→- **Impact**: When a user provides an invalid proxy configuration, the debug log shows useless literal text like `"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}"` instead of the actual invalid value. This makes debugging proxy issues unnecessarily difficult. Additionally, on line 547, `proxy_port` is not even defined in the `except ValueError` scope (the assignment `proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128` is what raised the `ValueError`), so adding the `f` prefix alone would produce a `NameError`. This is a double bug: a missing f-prefix masking a missing variable.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
    18→  if not proxy_host:
    19→      logger.info("Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}")
    20→      return False
    21→
    22→  try:
    23→      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
    24→  except ValueError:
    25→      logger.info("Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_port}")
    26→      return False
    27→  ```
    28→- **Recommended fix**:
    29→  ```python
    30→  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
    31→  if not proxy_host:
    32→      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {parsed.hostname!r}")
    33→      return False
    34→
    35→  try:
    36→      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
    37→  except ValueError:
    38→      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_env}")
    39→      return False
    40→  ```
    41→- **Historical precedent**: AP-002 is one of the most common bug patterns across charm repositories. The double-bug variant (missing f-prefix masking a NameError) is less common but has been seen in vault-k8s-operator.
    42→
    43→#### [BUG-002] Pebble -- Unguarded remove_path on known_hosts file crashes on first SSH clone (High)
    44→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:513`
    45→- **Pattern**: AP-006 variant (Pebble operation without existence guard)
    46→- **Issue**: `self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)` is called without first checking whether the file exists. On first run (or after the container is recreated), `/etc/git-secret/known_hosts` does not exist, and Pebble's `remove_path` raises `PathError` for non-existent paths.
    47→- **Impact**: The first time a user configures an SSH key for a private git repository, `_trust_ssh_remote` crashes the charm hook with an unhandled `PathError`. The charm enters error state and requires `juju resolve` to recover. Subsequent runs succeed because `push` on line 514 would have created the file (if the crash hadn't prevented reaching it). Compare with line 528-529 in `_push_ssh_config` which correctly checks `self.container.exists()` before calling `remove_path`.
    48→- **Evidence**:
    49→  ```python
    50→  # Line 513 -- no existence check before remove_path
    51→  self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)
    52→  self.container.push(self._known_hosts_file, stdout, make_dirs=True)
    53→  ```
    54→  Contrast with the correct pattern at line 528-529:
    55→  ```python
    56→  if self.container.exists(self._ssh_config_file):
    57→      self.container.remove_path(self._ssh_config_file)
    58→  ```
    59→- **Recommended fix**:
    60→  ```python
    61→  if self.container.exists(self._known_hosts_file):
    62→      self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)
    63→  self.container.push(self._known_hosts_file, stdout, make_dirs=True)
    64→  ```
    65→- **Historical precedent**: Missing existence checks before `remove_path` have been fixed in traefik-k8s-operator and other charms. The charm itself demonstrates awareness of this pattern at line 528.
    66→
    67→#### [BUG-003] Status -- BlockedStatus message has a typo (Medium)
    68→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:228-229`
    69→- **Pattern**: Novel
    70→- **Issue**: The BlockedStatus message reads `"Invalid proxy configuration - use see debug-log"` which contains a grammatical error (`"use see"`). This appears to be a copy-paste or editing mistake combining two phrasings: `"use debug-log"` and `"see debug-log"`.
    71→- **Impact**: Operators see a confusing status message when proxy configuration is invalid. While not a runtime bug, incorrect status messages are an operational issue since operators rely on status text to diagnose problems.
    72→- **Evidence**:
    73→  ```python
    74→  self.unit.status = BlockedStatus(
    75→      "Invalid proxy configuration - use see debug-log"
    76→  )
    77→  ```
    78→- **Recommended fix**:
    79→  ```python
    80→  self.unit.status = BlockedStatus(
    81→      "Invalid proxy configuration - see debug-log"
    82→  )
    83→  ```
    84→
    85→### Confirmed Safe
    86→
    87→- **Leader guard on `_stored_set`**: The `_stored_set` method (line 429) correctly checks `self.unit.is_leader()` before writing to app peer relation data.
    88→- **`can_connect()` guard in `_common_exit_hook`**: All Pebble operations flow through `_common_exit_hook` which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 208. The SSH-related Pebble calls in `_trust_ssh_remote`, `_push_ssh_config`, and `_save_ssh_key` are all reached only after this guard.
    89→- **`event.fail()` followed by `return`** in `_on_sync_now_action`: All three `event.fail()` calls (lines 274, 277, 288) are immediately followed by `return`.
    90→- **Config key names**: All `self.config.get()` keys (`git_repo`, `git_branch`, `git_rev`, `git_depth`, `git_ssh_key`, `git_ssh_key_secret`, `prometheus_alert_rules_path`, `loki_alert_rules_path`, `grafana_dashboards_path`, `slos_path`) match the keys defined in `charmcraft.yaml`.
    91→- **Truthiness check on `git_repo`**: `bool(self.config.get("git_repo"))` at line 306 is correct -- an empty string URL genuinely means "not configured".
    92→- **Truthiness check on `git_ssh_key`/`git_ssh_key_secret`**: Lines 224 and 382 use `if self.config.get("git_ssh_key")` which is safe because an empty string SSH key is genuinely unconfigured.
    93→- **No relation-broken handlers needed**: The charm provides relations (prometheus-config, grafana-dashboards, sloth) rather than requiring them, and uses library providers that handle relation lifecycle internally. The loki-config and tracing requires are handled by library consumers. No manual broken handler is needed.
    94→- **`container.exec` with `wait_output()`**: Both `_exec_sync_repo` (line 331) and `_trust_ssh_remote` (line 510) correctly call `wait_output()` on the exec process.
    95→

## Bug Review: cos-configuration-k8s-operator

### Summary
- **Findings**: 3 (0 Critical, 1 High, 2 Medium)
- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, configuration handling, status management, relation data, error handling, actions, URL/network, security
- **Charm name**: cos-configuration-k8s

### Findings

#### [BUG-001] Configuration -- Missing f-string prefix in proxy error logging (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:540` and `src/charm.py:547`
- **Pattern**: AP-002 (Missing f-string prefix)
- **Issue**: Two `logger.info()` calls are missing the `f` prefix, so they log the literal strings `{proxy_host}` and `{proxy_port}` instead of the actual values.
- **Impact**: When a user provides an invalid proxy configuration, the debug log shows useless literal text like `"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}"` instead of the actual invalid value. This makes debugging proxy issues unnecessarily difficult. Additionally, on line 547, `proxy_port` is not even defined in the `except ValueError` scope (the assignment `proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128` is what raised the `ValueError`), so adding the `f` prefix alone would produce a `NameError`. This is a double bug: a missing f-prefix masking a missing variable.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}")
      return False

  try:
      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_port}")
      return False
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:
      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {parsed.hostname!r}")
      return False

  try:
      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_env}")
      return False
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: AP-002 is one of the most common bug patterns across charm repositories. The double-bug variant (missing f-prefix masking a NameError) is less common but has been seen in vault-k8s-operator.

#### [BUG-002] Pebble -- Unguarded remove_path on known_hosts file crashes on first SSH clone (High)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:513`
- **Pattern**: AP-006 variant (Pebble operation without existence guard)
- **Issue**: `self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)` is called without first checking whether the file exists. On first run (or after the container is recreated), `/etc/git-secret/known_hosts` does not exist, and Pebble's `remove_path` raises `PathError` for non-existent paths.
- **Impact**: The first time a user configures an SSH key for a private git repository, `_trust_ssh_remote` crashes the charm hook with an unhandled `PathError`. The charm enters error state and requires `juju resolve` to recover. Subsequent runs succeed because `push` on line 514 would have created the file (if the crash hadn't prevented reaching it). Compare with line 528-529 in `_push_ssh_config` which correctly checks `self.container.exists()` before calling `remove_path`.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  # Line 513 -- no existence check before remove_path
  self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)
  self.container.push(self._known_hosts_file, stdout, make_dirs=True)
  ```
  Contrast with the correct pattern at line 528-529:
  ```python
  if self.container.exists(self._ssh_config_file):
      self.container.remove_path(self._ssh_config_file)
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  if self.container.exists(self._known_hosts_file):
      self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)
  self.container.push(self._known_hosts_file, stdout, make_dirs=True)
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: Missing existence checks before `remove_path` have been fixed in traefik-k8s-operator and other charms. The charm itself demonstrates awareness of this pattern at line 528.

#### [BUG-003] Status -- BlockedStatus message has a typo (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:228-229`
- **Pattern**: Novel
- **Issue**: The BlockedStatus message reads `"Invalid proxy configuration - use see debug-log"` which contains a grammatical error (`"use see"`). This appears to be a copy-paste or editing mistake combining two phrasings: `"use debug-log"` and `"see debug-log"`.
- **Impact**: Operators see a confusing status message when proxy configuration is invalid. While not a runtime bug, incorrect status messages are an operational issue since operators rely on status text to diagnose problems.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  self.unit.status = BlockedStatus(
      "Invalid proxy configuration - use see debug-log"
  )
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  self.unit.status = BlockedStatus(
      "Invalid proxy configuration - see debug-log"
  )
  ```

### Confirmed Safe

- **Leader guard on `_stored_set`**: The `_stored_set` method (line 429) correctly checks `self.unit.is_leader()` before writing to app peer relation data.
- **`can_connect()` guard in `_common_exit_hook`**: All Pebble operations flow through `_common_exit_hook` which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 208. The SSH-related Pebble calls in `_trust_ssh_remote`, `_push_ssh_config`, and `_save_ssh_key` are all reached only after this guard.
- **`event.fail()` followed by `return`** in `_on_sync_now_action`: All three `event.fail()` calls (lines 274, 277, 288) are immediately followed by `return`.
- **Config key names**: All `self.config.get()` keys (`git_repo`, `git_branch`, `git_rev`, `git_depth`, `git_ssh_key`, `git_ssh_key_secret`, `prometheus_alert_rules_path`, `loki_alert_rules_path`, `grafana_dashboards_path`, `slos_path`) match the keys defined in `charmcraft.yaml`.
- **Truthiness check on `git_repo`**: `bool(self.config.get("git_repo"))` at line 306 is correct -- an empty string URL genuinely means "not configured".
- **Truthiness check on `git_ssh_key`/`git_ssh_key_secret`**: Lines 224 and 382 use `if self.config.get("git_ssh_key")` which is safe because an empty string SSH key is genuinely unconfigured.
- **No relation-broken handlers needed**: The charm provides relations (prometheus-config, grafana-dashboards, sloth) rather than requiring them, and uses library providers that handle relation lifecycle internally. The loki-config and tracing requires are handled by library consumers. No manual broken handler is needed.
- **`container.exec` with `wait_output()`**: Both `_exec_sync_repo` (line 331) and `_trust_ssh_remote` (line 510) correctly call `wait_output()` on the exec process.

content.text: The cos-configuration one is the only finding where the *recommended fix* suggests adding `f` to a logger call. The others are just quoting existing code that already has `f` strings. Let me fix the source data for that one finding, then regenerate.

input.old_string:
#### [BUG-001] Configuration -- Missing f-string prefix in proxy error logging (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:540` and `src/charm.py:547`
- **Pattern**: AP-002 (Missing f-string prefix)
- **Issue**: Two `logger.info()` calls are missing the `f` prefix, so they log the literal strings `{proxy_host}` and `{proxy_port}` instead of the actual values.
- **Impact**: When a user provides an invalid proxy configuration, the debug log shows useless literal text like `"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}"` instead of the actual invalid value. This makes debugging proxy issues unnecessarily difficult. Additionally, on line 547, `proxy_port` is not even defined in the `except ValueError` scope (the assignment `proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128` is what raised the `ValueError`), so adding the `f` prefix alone would produce a `NameError`. This is a double bug: a missing f-prefix masking a missing variable.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}")
      return False

  try:
      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_port}")
      return False
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:
      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {parsed.hostname!r}")
      return False

  try:
      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_env}")
      return False
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: AP-002 is one of the most common bug patterns across charm repositories. The double-bug variant (missing f-prefix masking a NameError) is less common but has been seen in vault-k8s-operator.

This is a double bug: missing interpolation masking a missing variable.

  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:

      return False

      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_port}")
      return False
  ```

  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy hostname in model config: %r", parsed.hostname)

      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy port in model config: %s", proxy_env)
      return False
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: AP-002 is one of the most common bug patterns across charm repositories. The double-bug variant (missing interpolation masking a NameError) is less common but has been seen in vault-k8s-operator.

## Bug Review: cos-configuration-k8s-operator

### Summary
- **Findings**: 3 (0 Critical, 1 High, 2 Medium)
- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, configuration handling, status management, relation data, error handling, actions, URL/network, security
- **Charm name**: cos-configuration-k8s

### Findings

#### [BUG-001] Configuration -- Missing f-string prefix in proxy error logging (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:540` and `src/charm.py:547`
- **Pattern**: AP-002 (Missing f-string prefix)
- **Issue**: Two `logger.info()` calls are missing the `f` prefix, so they log the literal strings `{proxy_host}` and `{proxy_port}` instead of the actual values.
- **Impact**: When a user provides an invalid proxy configuration, the debug log shows useless literal text like `"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}"` instead of the actual invalid value. This makes debugging proxy issues unnecessarily difficult. Additionally, on line 547, `proxy_port` is not even defined in the `except ValueError` scope (the assignment `proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128` is what raised the `ValueError`), so adding the `f` prefix alone would produce a `NameError`. This is a double bug: a missing f-prefix masking a missing variable.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {proxy_host}")
      return False

  try:
      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info("Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_port}")
      return False
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  proxy_host = parsed.hostname
  if not proxy_host:
      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy hostname in model config: {parsed.hostname!r}")
      return False

  try:
      proxy_port = parsed.port or 3128
  except ValueError:
      logger.info(f"Invalid proxy port in model config: {proxy_env}")
      return False
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: AP-002 is one of the most common bug patterns across charm repositories. The double-bug variant (missing f-prefix masking a NameError) is less common but has been seen in vault-k8s-operator.

#### [BUG-002] Pebble -- Unguarded remove_path on known_hosts file crashes on first SSH clone (High)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:513`
- **Pattern**: AP-006 variant (Pebble operation without existence guard)
- **Issue**: `self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)` is called without first checking whether the file exists. On first run (or after the container is recreated), `/etc/git-secret/known_hosts` does not exist, and Pebble's `remove_path` raises `PathError` for non-existent paths.
- **Impact**: The first time a user configures an SSH key for a private git repository, `_trust_ssh_remote` crashes the charm hook with an unhandled `PathError`. The charm enters error state and requires `juju resolve` to recover. Subsequent runs succeed because `push` on line 514 would have created the file (if the crash hadn't prevented reaching it). Compare with line 528-529 in `_push_ssh_config` which correctly checks `self.container.exists()` before calling `remove_path`.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  # Line 513 -- no existence check before remove_path
  self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)
  self.container.push(self._known_hosts_file, stdout, make_dirs=True)
  ```
  Contrast with the correct pattern at line 528-529:
  ```python
  if self.container.exists(self._ssh_config_file):
      self.container.remove_path(self._ssh_config_file)
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  if self.container.exists(self._known_hosts_file):
      self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)
  self.container.push(self._known_hosts_file, stdout, make_dirs=True)
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: Missing existence checks before `remove_path` have been fixed in traefik-k8s-operator and other charms. The charm itself demonstrates awareness of this pattern at line 528.

#### [BUG-003] Status -- BlockedStatus message has a typo (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:228-229`
- **Pattern**: Novel
- **Issue**: The BlockedStatus message reads `"Invalid proxy configuration - use see debug-log"` which contains a grammatical error (`"use see"`). This appears to be a copy-paste or editing mistake combining two phrasings: `"use debug-log"` and `"see debug-log"`.
- **Impact**: Operators see a confusing status message when proxy configuration is invalid. While not a runtime bug, incorrect status messages are an operational issue since operators rely on status text to diagnose problems.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  self.unit.status = BlockedStatus(
      "Invalid proxy configuration - use see debug-log"
  )
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  self.unit.status = BlockedStatus(
      "Invalid proxy configuration - see debug-log"
  )
  ```

### Confirmed Safe

- **Leader guard on `_stored_set`**: The `_stored_set` method (line 429) correctly checks `self.unit.is_leader()` before writing to app peer relation data.
- **`can_connect()` guard in `_common_exit_hook`**: All Pebble operations flow through `_common_exit_hook` which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 208. The SSH-related Pebble calls in `_trust_ssh_remote`, `_push_ssh_config`, and `_save_ssh_key` are all reached only after this guard.
- **`event.fail()` followed by `return`** in `_on_sync_now_action`: All three `event.fail()` calls (lines 274, 277, 288) are immediately followed by `return`.
- **Config key names**: All `self.config.get()` keys (`git_repo`, `git_branch`, `git_rev`, `git_depth`, `git_ssh_key`, `git_ssh_key_secret`, `prometheus_alert_rules_path`, `loki_alert_rules_path`, `grafana_dashboards_path`, `slos_path`) match the keys defined in `charmcraft.yaml`.
- **Truthiness check on `git_repo`**: `bool(self.config.get("git_repo"))` at line 306 is correct -- an empty string URL genuinely means "not configured".
- **Truthiness check on `git_ssh_key`/`git_ssh_key_secret`**: Lines 224 and 382 use `if self.config.get("git_ssh_key")` which is safe because an empty string SSH key is genuinely unconfigured.
- **No relation-broken handlers needed**: The charm provides relations (prometheus-config, grafana-dashboards, sloth) rather than requiring them, and uses library providers that handle relation lifecycle internally. The loki-config and tracing requires are handled by library consumers. No manual broken handler is needed.
- **`container.exec` with `wait_output()`**: Both `_exec_sync_repo` (line 331) and `_trust_ssh_remote` (line 510) correctly call `wait_output()` on the exec process.

content.content:
# Bug Findings: cos-configuration-k8s-operator

## Background

I've been doing some experimentation into building a skill that finds bugs in charms, based on all the bugs that have already been fixed. You don't need to read all the gory details, but they are in the [Charming with Claude](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude) experiment "2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data" if you do.

After validating the skill against repos not used in its construction, I ran it across 42 repositories. The findings below are the results for **cos-configuration-k8s-operator**. Each finding includes the location, a description of the issue, its impact, the relevant code, and a suggested fix.

I have not verified these findings against the running charm — they are based on static analysis of the source code at the time of review. Some may be mitigated by runtime conditions not visible in the code, or may have already been addressed.

I don't want to open a bunch of issues or PRs in your repo with what might turn out to be AI slop. On the other hand, if there are actual bugs here, it would be good to solve them. Also, somewhat selfishly, I would love extra data on any false positives to feed back into the skill, to try to make it something actually useful across charming.

Basically: the ball is in your court. You can ignore these, you can review them and open issues yourself (in many cases Copilot should be able to provide a draft fix; these aren't complex issues). You can ask me to open issues and/or PRs.

(If there's anything that looks to me (not just AI) like a security finding, they were excluded and I'll handle them separately.)

## Summary

- **Findings**: 3 (1 High, 2 Medium)
- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, configuration handling, status management, relation data, error handling, actions, URL/network, security

Note that the severity and area were picked by AI and I don't necessarily agree with them all, but they're a handy base classification.

## Findings

### High

#### BUG-002: Unguarded remove_path on known_hosts file crashes on first SSH clone

**Location**: [`src/charm.py:513`](https://github.com/canonical/cos-configuration-k8s-operator/blob/main/src/charm.py#L513)

**Issue**: `self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)` is called without first checking whether the file exists. On first run (or after the container is recreated), `/etc/git-secret/known_hosts` does not exist, and Pebble's `remove_path` raises `PathError` for non-existent paths.

**Impact**: The first time a user configures an SSH key for a private git repository, `_trust_ssh_remote` crashes the charm hook with an unhandled `PathError`. The charm enters error state and requires `juju resolve` to recover. Subsequent runs succeed because `push` on line 514 would have created the file (if the crash hadn't prevented reaching it). Compare with line 528-529 in `_push_ssh_config` which correctly checks `self.container.exists()` before calling `remove_path`.

**Code**: 
  ```python
  # Line 513 -- no existence check before remove_path
  self.container.remove_path(self._known_hosts_file, recursive=True)
  self.container.push(self._known_hosts_file, stdout, make_dirs=True)
  ```
  Contrast with the correct pattern at line 528-529:
  ```python
  if self.container.exists(self._ssh_config_file):
      self.container.remove_path(self._ssh_config_file)
  ```

**Recommended fix**: 
  ```python
  if self.container.exists(self._known_hosts_file):

content.content:
1→## Bug Review: discourse-k8s-operator src/ directory
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 4 (0 Critical, 2 High, 2 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/containers, Relations/events, Configuration handling, Status management, Ingress/networking, Observability/Grafana, Auth/identity (SAML, OAuth)
     6→- **Charm name**: discourse-k8s
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Observability -- Grafana dashboard uses non-COS datasource variable (High)
    11→- **Location**: `src/grafana_dashboards/discourse.json:4,61,139,217,295,373,451,538,640,754,858,972,1057,1146,1223,1320,1397,1486,1563,1660,1737,1826,1903`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-022 (Wrong datasource variable)
    13→- **Issue**: The `discourse.json` dashboard uses `${DS_PROMETHEUS}` as its datasource template variable throughout the file, instead of the COS-standard `${prometheusds}`. The companion dashboard `discourse_service_health.json` correctly uses `${prometheusds}`.
    14→- **Impact**: All panels in `discourse.json` fail to render in COS Grafana because no datasource named `DS_PROMETHEUS` exists. The dashboard appears blank. This affects 23 datasource references across the entire dashboard.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```json
    17→  "__inputs": [
    18→    {
    19→      "name": "DS_PROMETHEUS",
    20→      "label": "Prometheus",
    21→      ...
    22→    }
    23→  ],
    24→  ...
    25→  "datasource": "${DS_PROMETHEUS}",
    26→  ```
    27→- **Recommended fix**:
    28→  Replace all occurrences of `DS_PROMETHEUS` with `prometheusds`:
    29→  ```json
    30→  "datasource": "${prometheusds}",
    31→  ```
    32→  Also remove the `__inputs` block, which is a Grafana export artifact not used by COS.
    33→- **Historical precedent**: This exact pattern was fixed in grafana-k8s, alertmanager-k8s, and many other charms. The anti-patterns reference identifies `DS_PROMETHEUS` as the most common Grafana dashboard bug in COS deployments.
    34→
    35→#### [BUG-002] Relations -- Missing SAML relation_broken handler leaves stale config (High)
    36→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:97` (handler registration area -- no broken handler registered for saml)
    37→- **Pattern**: AP-012 (Missing relation_broken handler)
    38→- **Issue**: The charm handles `saml_data_available` (line 97) to configure SAML environment variables, but there is no `saml_relation_broken` handler. When the SAML relation is removed, the SAML-related environment variables (`DISCOURSE_SAML_TARGET_URL`, `DISCOURSE_SAML_CERT_FINGERPRINT`, `DISCOURSE_SAML_FULL_SCREEN_LOGIN`, etc.) persist in the Pebble layer until the next unrelated event triggers `_configure_pod`. During this window, Discourse continues to enforce SAML authentication using stale IdP settings.
    39→- **Impact**: After removing the SAML relation, users may be redirected to a now-invalid SAML identity provider for login, locking them out until another event triggers a full reconfiguration. Contrast with the OAuth relation, which has a proper `relation_broken` handler (line 58 in `oauth_observer.py`).
    40→- **Evidence**:
    41→  ```python
    42→  # SAML relation setup (line 97) -- no corresponding broken handler
    43→  self.saml = SamlRequires(self)
    44→  self.framework.observe(self.saml.on.saml_data_available, self._on_saml_data_available)
    45→  # No: self.framework.observe(self.on[DEFAULT_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, ...)
    46→
    47→  # Compare with OAuth which correctly handles broken (oauth_observer.py:57-59):
    48→  self.framework.observe(
    49→      self.charm.on[OAUTH_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, self._on_oauth_relation_broken
    50→  )
    51→  ```
    52→- **Recommended fix**:
    53→  ```python
    54→  self.framework.observe(
    55→      self.on[DEFAULT_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, self._on_saml_relation_broken
    56→  )
    57→
    58→  def _on_saml_relation_broken(self, _: RelationBrokenEvent) -> None:
    59→      """Handle SAML relation broken -- reconfigure to remove stale SAML settings."""
    60→      self._configure_pod()
    61→  ```
    62→
    63→#### [BUG-003] Pebble -- Unconditional replan causes unnecessary service restarts (Medium)
    64→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:928-935`
    65→- **Pattern**: AP-008 (Unconditional replan)
    66→- **Issue**: `_start_service` calls `container.add_layer()` and `container.pebble.replan_services()` on every invocation without comparing the current plan to the new layer. This method is called from `_activate_charm`, which is reached from `_setup_and_activate` (start, pebble_ready, database_created, redis_changed, rolling_restart, upgrade) and `_configure_pod` (config_changed, saml_data_available). Every one of these events causes a full service restart even when no configuration has actually changed.
    67→- **Impact**: Discourse is a Ruby on Rails application with significant startup time. Unnecessary restarts drop in-flight HTTP requests and cause brief outages. In a multi-unit deployment with frequent relation events, this compounds.
    68→- **Evidence**:
    69→  ```python
    70→  def _start_service(self):
    71→      """Start discourse."""
    72→      logger.info("Starting discourse")
    73→      container = self.unit.get_container(CONTAINER_NAME)
    74→      if self._is_config_valid() and container.can_connect():
    75→          layer_config = self._create_layer_config()
    76→          container.add_layer(SERVICE_NAME, layer_config, combine=True)
    77→          container.pebble.replan_services()  # Always restarts, no plan comparison
    78→  ```
    79→- **Recommended fix**:
    80→  ```python
    81→  def _start_service(self):
    82→      """Start discourse."""
    83→      logger.info("Starting discourse")
    84→      container = self.unit.get_container(CONTAINER_NAME)
    85→      if self._is_config_valid() and container.can_connect():
    86→          layer_config = self._create_layer_config()
    87→          container.add_layer(SERVICE_NAME, layer_config, combine=True)
    88→          new_plan = container.get_plan()
    89→          if new_plan.services != ops.pebble.Layer(layer_config).services:
    90→              container.pebble.replan_services()
    91→  ```
    92→- **Historical precedent**: This exact pattern was fixed in alertmanager-k8s, grafana-k8s, loki-k8s, prometheus-k8s, and many other charms.
    93→
    94→#### [BUG-004] Error Handling -- Unhandled StopIteration in SAML endpoint lookup (Medium)
    95→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:346-351`
    96→- **Pattern**: Novel (exception handling gap)
    97→- **Issue**: `_get_saml_config` uses `next()` on a generator expression without a default value to find the SSO redirect endpoint. If the SAML relation data does not contain a `SingleSignOnService` endpoint with binding `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect`, the `next()` call raises `StopIteration`, which is unhandled and crashes the charm.
    98→- **Impact**: If the SAML integrator provides relation data that is missing the expected endpoint (e.g., during a partial update, misconfiguration, or if using a non-standard IdP), the charm crashes on any event that triggers `_create_discourse_environment_settings`. The `StopIteration` exception is particularly dangerous because, if this code were ever called from within a generator context, it would silently terminate iteration rather than raising an error.
    99→- **Evidence**:
   100→  ```python
   101→  sso_redirect_endpoint = next(
   102→      e
   103→      for e in relation_data.endpoints
   104→      if e.name == "SingleSignOnService"
   105→      and e.binding == "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect"
   106→  )
   107→  # No default argument -- raises StopIteration if no match
   108→  ```
   109→- **Recommended fix**:
   110→  ```python
   111→  sso_redirect_endpoint = next(
   112→      (
   113→          e
   114→          for e in relation_data.endpoints
   115→          if e.name == "SingleSignOnService"
   116→          and e.binding == "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect"
   117→      ),
   118→      None,
   119→  )
   120→  if sso_redirect_endpoint is None:
   121→      logger.warning("SAML relation data missing SingleSignOnService redirect endpoint")
   122→      return {}
   123→  ```
   124→
   125→### Confirmed Safe
   126→
   127→| Pattern | Location | Why it's safe |
   128→|---------|----------|---------------|
   129→| `if self.config["external_hostname"]` truthiness check | `charm.py:227` | Config type is `string` with default `""`. The empty-string fallback to `self.app.name` is the intended behavior. No valid hostname is falsy. |
   130→| `container.push()` in `_set_setup_completed` without direct `can_connect()` | `charm.py:273` | Called only from `_set_up_discourse`, which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 660. |
   131→| `container.exec()` in `_user_exists` and `_activate_user` without `can_connect()` | `charm.py:738,759` | Both are only called from action handlers (`_on_promote_user_action`, `_on_create_user_action`) which check `can_connect()` at the handler entry point. |
   132→| `container.exec()` in `_config_force_https` without `can_connect()` | `charm.py:883` | Called from `_configure_pod` at line 715, which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 714 immediately before. |
   133→| `relation.data[relation.app]` in `_get_redis_relation_data` | `charm.py:418` | Accessed via `model.get_relation()` (not from an event), and preceded by a truthiness check on `relation` at line 416. The `relation.app` from `model.get_relation()` is always set for established relations. |
   134→| `if not self.config[s3_config]` truthiness check for S3 settings | `charm.py:327` | All required S3 settings are `type: string`. Empty string correctly means "not configured". No valid value is falsy. |
   135→| `THROTTLE_LEVELS.get(self.config["throttle_level"])` potentially returning None | `charm.py:491` | While `.get()` on a `defaultdict` can return `None` for unknown keys, all callers go through `_is_config_valid()` first (via `_configure_pod`), which validates the throttle level. The `_execute_migrations` path also goes through `_are_relations_ready` before reaching this code, and invalid config would have been caught on a prior `config_changed` event. Marking as safe because the crash requires a very specific race: invalid throttle_level set exactly when the first `database_created` event fires, before any `config_changed` event has run validation. |
   136→| No explicit `redis_relation_broken` handler | `charm.py` | `_are_relations_ready()` checks `self.redis.relation_data` and sets `WaitingStatus` if empty. While there is no immediate cleanup on broken, the next event triggers the readiness check. The redis relation has `limit: 1` in metadata.yaml. |
   137→| `discourse_service_health.json` datasource variable | `src/grafana_dashboards/discourse_service_health.json` | Correctly uses `${prometheusds}` throughout. |
   138→| OAuth relation handler structure | `src/oauth_observer.py` | Has both `relation_changed` and `relation_broken` handlers. Correctly clears `client_config` on broken and calls `_setup_and_activate_callback`. |
   139→

## Bug Review: discourse-k8s-operator src/ directory

### Summary
- **Findings**: 4 (0 Critical, 2 High, 2 Medium)
- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/containers, Relations/events, Configuration handling, Status management, Ingress/networking, Observability/Grafana, Auth/identity (SAML, OAuth)
- **Charm name**: discourse-k8s

### Findings

#### [BUG-001] Observability -- Grafana dashboard uses non-COS datasource variable (High)
- **Location**: `src/grafana_dashboards/discourse.json:4,61,139,217,295,373,451,538,640,754,858,972,1057,1146,1223,1320,1397,1486,1563,1660,1737,1826,1903`
- **Pattern**: AP-022 (Wrong datasource variable)
- **Issue**: The `discourse.json` dashboard uses `${DS_PROMETHEUS}` as its datasource template variable throughout the file, instead of the COS-standard `${prometheusds}`. The companion dashboard `discourse_service_health.json` correctly uses `${prometheusds}`.
- **Impact**: All panels in `discourse.json` fail to render in COS Grafana because no datasource named `DS_PROMETHEUS` exists. The dashboard appears blank. This affects 23 datasource references across the entire dashboard.
- **Evidence**:
  ```json
  "__inputs": [
    {
      "name": "DS_PROMETHEUS",
      "label": "Prometheus",
      ...
    }
  ],
  ...
  "datasource": "${DS_PROMETHEUS}",
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  Replace all occurrences of `DS_PROMETHEUS` with `prometheusds`:
  ```json
  "datasource": "${prometheusds}",
  ```
  Also remove the `__inputs` block, which is a Grafana export artifact not used by COS.
- **Historical precedent**: This exact pattern was fixed in grafana-k8s, alertmanager-k8s, and many other charms. The anti-patterns reference identifies `DS_PROMETHEUS` as the most common Grafana dashboard bug in COS deployments.

#### [BUG-002] Relations -- Missing SAML relation_broken handler leaves stale config (High)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:97` (handler registration area -- no broken handler registered for saml)
- **Pattern**: AP-012 (Missing relation_broken handler)
- **Issue**: The charm handles `saml_data_available` (line 97) to configure SAML environment variables, but there is no `saml_relation_broken` handler. When the SAML relation is removed, the SAML-related environment variables (`DISCOURSE_SAML_TARGET_URL`, `DISCOURSE_SAML_CERT_FINGERPRINT`, `DISCOURSE_SAML_FULL_SCREEN_LOGIN`, etc.) persist in the Pebble layer until the next unrelated event triggers `_configure_pod`. During this window, Discourse continues to enforce SAML authentication using stale IdP settings.
- **Impact**: After removing the SAML relation, users may be redirected to a now-invalid SAML identity provider for login, locking them out until another event triggers a full reconfiguration. Contrast with the OAuth relation, which has a proper `relation_broken` handler (line 58 in `oauth_observer.py`).
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  # SAML relation setup (line 97) -- no corresponding broken handler
  self.saml = SamlRequires(self)
  self.framework.observe(self.saml.on.saml_data_available, self._on_saml_data_available)
  # No: self.framework.observe(self.on[DEFAULT_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, ...)

  # Compare with OAuth which correctly handles broken (oauth_observer.py:57-59):
  self.framework.observe(
      self.charm.on[OAUTH_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, self._on_oauth_relation_broken
  )
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  self.framework.observe(
      self.on[DEFAULT_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, self._on_saml_relation_broken
  )

  def _on_saml_relation_broken(self, _: RelationBrokenEvent) -> None:
      """Handle SAML relation broken -- reconfigure to remove stale SAML settings."""
      self._configure_pod()
  ```

#### [BUG-003] Pebble -- Unconditional replan causes unnecessary service restarts (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:928-935`
- **Pattern**: AP-008 (Unconditional replan)
- **Issue**: `_start_service` calls `container.add_layer()` and `container.pebble.replan_services()` on every invocation without comparing the current plan to the new layer. This method is called from `_activate_charm`, which is reached from `_setup_and_activate` (start, pebble_ready, database_created, redis_changed, rolling_restart, upgrade) and `_configure_pod` (config_changed, saml_data_available). Every one of these events causes a full service restart even when no configuration has actually changed.
- **Impact**: Discourse is a Ruby on Rails application with significant startup time. Unnecessary restarts drop in-flight HTTP requests and cause brief outages. In a multi-unit deployment with frequent relation events, this compounds.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  def _start_service(self):
      """Start discourse."""
      logger.info("Starting discourse")
      container = self.unit.get_container(CONTAINER_NAME)
      if self._is_config_valid() and container.can_connect():
          layer_config = self._create_layer_config()
          container.add_layer(SERVICE_NAME, layer_config, combine=True)
          container.pebble.replan_services()  # Always restarts, no plan comparison
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  def _start_service(self):
      """Start discourse."""
      logger.info("Starting discourse")
      container = self.unit.get_container(CONTAINER_NAME)
      if self._is_config_valid() and container.can_connect():
          layer_config = self._create_layer_config()
          container.add_layer(SERVICE_NAME, layer_config, combine=True)
          new_plan = container.get_plan()
          if new_plan.services != ops.pebble.Layer(layer_config).services:
              container.pebble.replan_services()
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: This exact pattern was fixed in alertmanager-k8s, grafana-k8s, loki-k8s, prometheus-k8s, and many other charms.

#### [BUG-004] Error Handling -- Unhandled StopIteration in SAML endpoint lookup (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:346-351`
- **Pattern**: Novel (exception handling gap)
- **Issue**: `_get_saml_config` uses `next()` on a generator expression without a default value to find the SSO redirect endpoint. If the SAML relation data does not contain a `SingleSignOnService` endpoint with binding `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect`, the `next()` call raises `StopIteration`, which is unhandled and crashes the charm.
- **Impact**: If the SAML integrator provides relation data that is missing the expected endpoint (e.g., during a partial update, misconfiguration, or if using a non-standard IdP), the charm crashes on any event that triggers `_create_discourse_environment_settings`. The `StopIteration` exception is particularly dangerous because, if this code were ever called from within a generator context, it would silently terminate iteration rather than raising an error.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  sso_redirect_endpoint = next(
      e
      for e in relation_data.endpoints
      if e.name == "SingleSignOnService"
      and e.binding == "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect"
  )
  # No default argument -- raises StopIteration if no match
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  sso_redirect_endpoint = next(
      (
          e
          for e in relation_data.endpoints
          if e.name == "SingleSignOnService"
          and e.binding == "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect"
      ),
      None,
  )
  if sso_redirect_endpoint is None:
      logger.warning("SAML relation data missing SingleSignOnService redirect endpoint")
      return {}
  ```

### Confirmed Safe

| Pattern | Location | Why it's safe |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| `if self.config["external_hostname"]` truthiness check | `charm.py:227` | Config type is `string` with default `""`. The empty-string fallback to `self.app.name` is the intended behavior. No valid hostname is falsy. |
| `container.push()` in `_set_setup_completed` without direct `can_connect()` | `charm.py:273` | Called only from `_set_up_discourse`, which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 660. |
| `container.exec()` in `_user_exists` and `_activate_user` without `can_connect()` | `charm.py:738,759` | Both are only called from action handlers (`_on_promote_user_action`, `_on_create_user_action`) which check `can_connect()` at the handler entry point. |
| `container.exec()` in `_config_force_https` without `can_connect()` | `charm.py:883` | Called from `_configure_pod` at line 715, which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 714 immediately before. |
| `relation.data[relation.app]` in `_get_redis_relation_data` | `charm.py:418` | Accessed via `model.get_relation()` (not from an event), and preceded by a truthiness check on `relation` at line 416. The `relation.app` from `model.get_relation()` is always set for established relations. |
| `if not self.config[s3_config]` truthiness check for S3 settings | `charm.py:327` | All required S3 settings are `type: string`. Empty string correctly means "not configured". No valid value is falsy. |
| `THROTTLE_LEVELS.get(self.config["throttle_level"])` potentially returning None | `charm.py:491` | While `.get()` on a `defaultdict` can return `None` for unknown keys, all callers go through `_is_config_valid()` first (via `_configure_pod`), which validates the throttle level. The `_execute_migrations` path also goes through `_are_relations_ready` before reaching this code, and invalid config would have been caught on a prior `config_changed` event. Marking as safe because the crash requires a very specific race: invalid throttle_level set exactly when the first `database_created` event fires, before any `config_changed` event has run validation. |
| No explicit `redis_relation_broken` handler | `charm.py` | `_are_relations_ready()` checks `self.redis.relation_data` and sets `WaitingStatus` if empty. While there is no immediate cleanup on broken, the next event triggers the readiness check. The redis relation has `limit: 1` in metadata.yaml. |
| `discourse_service_health.json` datasource variable | `src/grafana_dashboards/discourse_service_health.json` | Correctly uses `${prometheusds}` throughout. |
| OAuth relation handler structure | `src/oauth_observer.py` | Has both `relation_changed` and `relation_broken` handlers. Correctly clears `client_config` on broken and calls `_setup_and_activate_callback`. |

content.text: BUG-003 is the unconditional replan finding. I'll remove it from the source data and regenerate.

  ...
  "datasource": "${DS_PROMETHEUS}",
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  Replace all occurrences of `DS_PROMETHEUS` with `prometheusds`:
  ```json
  "datasource": "${prometheusds}",
  ```
  Also remove the `__inputs` block, which is a Grafana export artifact not used by COS.
- **Historical precedent**: This exact pattern was fixed in grafana-k8s, alertmanager-k8s, and many other charms. The anti-patterns reference identifies `DS_PROMETHEUS` as the most common Grafana dashboard bug in COS deployments.

#### [BUG-002] Relations -- Missing SAML relation_broken handler leaves stale config (High)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:97` (handler registration area -- no broken handler registered for saml)
- **Pattern**: AP-012 (Missing relation_broken handler)
- **Issue**: The charm handles `saml_data_available` (line 97) to configure SAML environment variables, but there is no `saml_relation_broken` handler. When the SAML relation is removed, the SAML-related environment variables (`DISCOURSE_SAML_TARGET_URL`, `DISCOURSE_SAML_CERT_FINGERPRINT`, `DISCOURSE_SAML_FULL_SCREEN_LOGIN`, etc.) persist in the Pebble layer until the next unrelated event triggers `_configure_pod`. During this window, Discourse continues to enforce SAML authentication using stale IdP settings.
- **Impact**: After removing the SAML relation, users may be redirected to a now-invalid SAML identity provider for login, locking them out until another event triggers a full reconfiguration. Contrast with the OAuth relation, which has a proper `relation_broken` handler (line 58 in `oauth_observer.py`).
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  # SAML relation setup (line 97) -- no corresponding broken handler
  self.saml = SamlRequires(self)
  self.framework.observe(self.saml.on.saml_data_available, self._on_saml_data_available)
  # No: self.framework.observe(self.on[DEFAULT_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, ...)

  # Compare with OAuth which correctly handles broken (oauth_observer.py:57-59):
  self.framework.observe(
      self.charm.on[OAUTH_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, self._on_oauth_relation_broken
  )
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  self.framework.observe(
      self.on[DEFAULT_RELATION_NAME].relation_broken, self._on_saml_relation_broken
  )

  def _on_saml_relation_broken(self, _: RelationBrokenEvent) -> None:
      """Handle SAML relation broken -- reconfigure to remove stale SAML settings."""
      self._configure_pod()
  ```

#### [BUG-003] Pebble -- Unconditional replan causes unnecessary service restarts (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:928-935`
- **Pattern**: AP-008 (Unconditional replan)
- **Issue**: `_start_service` calls `container.add_layer()` and `container.pebble.replan_services()` on every invocation without comparing the current plan to the new layer. This method is called from `_activate_charm`, which is reached from `_setup_and_activate` (start, pebble_ready, database_created, redis_changed, rolling_restart, upgrade) and `_configure_pod` (config_changed, saml_data_available). Every one of these events causes a full service restart even when no configuration has actually changed.
- **Impact**: Discourse is a Ruby on Rails application with significant startup time. Unnecessary restarts drop in-flight HTTP requests and cause brief outages. In a multi-unit deployment with frequent relation events, this compounds.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  def _start_service(self):
      """Start discourse."""
      logger.info("Starting discourse")
      container = self.unit.get_container(CONTAINER_NAME)
      if self._is_config_valid() and container.can_connect():
          layer_config = self._create_layer_config()
          container.add_layer(SERVICE_NAME, layer_config, combine=True)
          container.pebble.replan_services()  # Always restarts, no plan comparison
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  def _start_service(self):
      """Start discourse."""
      logger.info("Starting discourse")
      container = self.unit.get_container(CONTAINER_NAME)
      if self._is_config_valid() and container.can_connect():
          layer_config = self._create_layer_config()
          container.add_layer(SERVICE_NAME, layer_config, combine=True)
          new_plan = container.get_plan()
          if new_plan.services != ops.pebble.Layer(layer_config).services:
              container.pebble.replan_services()
  ```
- **Historical precedent**: This exact pattern was fixed in alertmanager-k8s, grafana-k8s, loki-k8s, prometheus-k8s, and many other charms.

#### [BUG-004] Error Handling -- Unhandled StopIteration in SAML endpoint lookup (Medium)
- **Location**: `src/charm.py:346-351`
- **Pattern**: Novel (exception handling gap)
- **Issue**: `_get_saml_config` uses `next()` on a generator expression without a default value to find the SSO redirect endpoint. If the SAML relation data does not contain a `SingleSignOnService` endpoint with binding `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect`, the `next()` call raises `StopIteration`, which is unhandled and crashes the charm.
- **Impact**: If the SAML integrator provides relation data that is missing the expected endpoint (e.g., during a partial update, misconfiguration, or if using a non-standard IdP), the charm crashes on any event that triggers `_create_discourse_environment_settings`. The `StopIteration` exception is particularly dangerous because, if this code were ever called from within a generator context, it would silently terminate iteration rather than raising an error.
- **Evidence**:
  ```python
  sso_redirect_endpoint = next(
      e
      for e in relation_data.endpoints
      if e.name == "SingleSignOnService"
      and e.binding == "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect"
  )
  # No default argument -- raises StopIteration if no match
  ```
- **Recommended fix**:
  ```python
  sso_redirect_endpoint = next(
      (
          e
          for e in relation_data.endpoints
          if e.name == "SingleSignOnService"
          and e.binding == "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:bindings:HTTP-Redirect"
      ),
      None,
  )
  if sso_redirect_endpoint is None:
      logger.warning("SAML relation data missing SingleSignOnService redirect endpoint")
      return {}
  ```

### Confirmed Safe

| Pattern | Location | Why it's safe |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| `if self.config["external_hostname"]` truthiness check | `charm.py:227` | Config type is `string` with default `""`. The empty-string fallback to `self.app.name` is the intended behavior. No valid hostname is falsy. |
| `container.push()` in `_set_setup_completed` without direct `can_connect()` | `charm.py:273` | Called only from `_set_up_discourse`, which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 660. |
| `container.exec()` in `_user_exists` and `_activate_user` without `can_connect()` | `charm.py:738,759` | Both are only called from action handlers (`_on_promote_user_action`, `_on_create_user_action`) which check `can_connect()` at the handler entry point. |
| `container.exec()` in `_config_force_https` without `can_connect()` | `charm.py:883` | Called from `_configure_pod` at line 715, which checks `container.can_connect()` at line 714 immediately before. |
| `relation.data[relation.app]` in `_get_redis_relation_data` | `charm.py:418` | Accessed via `model.get_relation()` (not from an event), and preceded by a truthiness check on `relation` at line 416. The `relation.app` from `model.get_relation()` is always set for established relations. |
| `if not self.config[s3_config]` truthiness check for S3 settings | `charm.py:327` | All required S3 settings are `type: string`. Empty string correctly means "not configured". No valid value is falsy. |
| `THROTTLE_LEVELS.get(self.config["throttle_level"])` potentially returning None | `charm.py:491` | While `.get()` on a `defaultdict` can return `None` for unknown keys, all callers go through `_is_config_valid()` first (via `_configure_pod`), which validates the throttle level. The `_execute_migrations` path also goes through `_are_relations_ready` before reaching this code, and invalid config would have been caught on a prior `config_changed` event. Marking as safe because the crash requires a very specific race: invalid throttle_level set exactly when the first `database_created` event fires, before any `config_changed` event has run validation. |
| No explicit `redis_relation_broken` handler | `charm.py` | `_are_relations_ready()` checks `self.redis.relation_data` and sets `WaitingStatus` if empty. While there is no immediate cleanup on broken, the next event triggers the readiness check. The redis relation has `limit: 1` in metadata.yaml. |
| `discourse_service_health.json` datasource variable | `src/grafana_dashboards/discourse_service_health.json` | Correctly uses `${prometheusds}` throughout. |
| OAuth relation handler structure | `src/oauth_observer.py` | Has both `relation_changed` and `relation_broken` handlers. Correctly clears `client_config` on broken and calls `_setup_and_activate_callback`. |

content.text: It's passed to `bash -c`, so bash will strip the quotes. This is a false positive — the quotes are harmless since bash processes them. Let me remove BUG-001 from the grafana-agent source data.
