#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate per-repo team writeup files from skill_validation_*.md data."""

import re
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

DATA_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "data"
REPOS_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "repos"
OUTPUT_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "writeups"

TOTAL_REPOS = 42  # total repos reviewed

TEMPLATE_INTRO = """\
# Bug Findings: {repo_name}

## Background

I've been doing some experimentation into building a skill that finds bugs in charms, based on all the bugs that have already been fixed. You don't need to read all the gory details, but they are in the [Charming with Claude](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude) experiment "2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data" if you do.

After validating the skill against repos not used in its construction, I ran it across {total_repos} repositories. The findings below are the results for **{repo_name}**. Each finding includes the location, a description of the issue, its impact, the relevant code, and a suggested fix.

I have not verified these findings against the running charm — they are based on static analysis of the source code at the time of review. Some may be mitigated by runtime conditions not visible in the code, or may have already been addressed.

I don't want to open a bunch of issues or PRs in your repo with what might turn out to be AI slop. On the other hand, if there are actual bugs here, it would be good to solve them. Also, somewhat selfishly, I would love extra data on any false positives to feed back into the skill, to try to make it something actually useful across charming.

Basically: the ball is in your court. You can ignore these, you can review them and open issues yourself (in many cases Copilot should be able to provide a draft fix; these aren't complex issues). You can ask me to open issues and/or PRs.

(If there's anything that looks to me (not just AI) like a security finding, they were excluded and I'll handle them separately.)

## Summary

- **Findings**: {total} ({severity_summary})

Note that the severity was picked by AI and I don't necessarily agree with all of them, but it's a handy base classification.

## Findings
"""

SEVERITY_ORDER = ["Critical", "High", "Medium", "Low"]

PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_NONE = (
    "\n\n> **Note**: `None` values in Pebble environment dicts do cause `add_layer` "
    "to fail. However, non-string values like `int`, `bool`, and `float` are "
    "silently coerced to strings by Pebble's Go YAML decoder (see "
    "[pebble-non-string-env.md](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude"
    "/blob/main/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis"
    "/pebble-non-string-env.md) for details). The `None` part of this finding is "
    "a real crash; the non-string part is a code quality issue with no runtime impact.\n"
)

PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_NON_STRING_ONLY = (
    "\n\n> **Note**: Pebble silently coerces non-string scalar values (`int`, `bool`, "
    "`float`) to strings via its Go YAML decoder — so this does not crash at runtime "
    "(see [pebble-non-string-env.md](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude"
    "/blob/main/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis"
    "/pebble-non-string-env.md) for details). "
    "It is still technically incorrect and relies on undocumented behaviour, but "
    "the practical impact is minimal.\n"
)

PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_BYTES = (
    "\n\n> **Note**: Non-string scalar values (`int`, `bool`, `float`) in Pebble "
    "environment dicts are silently coerced to strings by Pebble's Go YAML decoder "
    "(see [pebble-non-string-env.md](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude"
    "/blob/main/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis"
    "/pebble-non-string-env.md) for details). "
    "However, `bytes` objects may not be handled the same way.\n"
)


def get_github_url(repo_dir_name: str) -> str | None:
    """Get the GitHub base URL for a repo."""
    repo_path = REPOS_DIR / repo_dir_name
    if not repo_path.exists():
        return None
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["git", "-C", str(repo_path), "remote", "get-url", "origin"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
        )
        url = result.stdout.strip().removesuffix(".git")
        # Get the default branch
        result2 = subprocess.run(
            ["git", "-C", str(repo_path), "rev-parse", "--abbrev-ref", "HEAD"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
        )
        branch = result2.stdout.strip()
        return f"{url}/blob/{branch}"
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError:
        return None


def get_repo_dir_name(repo_name: str) -> str | None:
    """Map the repo name from the validation file to the directory name in repos/."""
    # Direct match
    if (REPOS_DIR / repo_name).exists():
        return repo_name

    # Try common variations
    variations = [
        repo_name,
        f"{repo_name}-operator",
        f"{repo_name}-k8s-operator",
    ]
    for v in variations:
        if (REPOS_DIR / v).exists():
            return v

    # Known mappings for tricky names
    known = {
        "grafana-agent": "grafana-agent-k8s-operator",
        "seldon-core": "seldon-core-operator",
        "zookeeper": "zookeeper-k8s-operator",
        "sdcore-amf-k8s-operator": "sdcore-amf-operator",
    }
    if repo_name in known and (REPOS_DIR / known[repo_name]).exists():
        return known[repo_name]

    # Fuzzy: check all repo dirs for a substring match
    core = repo_name.replace("-operator", "").replace("-k8s", "")
    for d in sorted(REPOS_DIR.iterdir()):
        if d.is_dir() and core in d.name:
            return d.name

    return None


def parse_validation_file(filepath: Path) -> dict:
    """Parse a skill_validation_*.md file into structured data."""
    text = filepath.read_text()

    # Extract repo name from header
    header_match = re.match(r"## Bug Review:\s*(.+)", text)
    repo_name = header_match.group(1).strip() if header_match else filepath.stem

    # Clean up repo name (remove " src/ directory", " src/", trailing slashes, parentheticals, etc.)
    repo_name = re.sub(r"\s*(src/?|src/ directory|--.*|\(.*\))", "", repo_name).strip()
    repo_name = repo_name.rstrip("/").strip()
    # Special cases
    repo_name = repo_name.replace("directory", "").strip()

    # Extract summary line
    findings_match = re.search(
        r"\*\*Findings\*\*:\s*(\d+)\s*\(([^)]+)\)", text
    )
    total = int(findings_match.group(1)) if findings_match else 0
    severity_summary = findings_match.group(2) if findings_match else ""

    # Extract areas
    areas_match = re.search(
        r"\*\*Code areas reviewed\*\*:\s*(.+)", text
    )
    areas = areas_match.group(1).strip() if areas_match else ""

    # Split into findings section and confirmed safe section
    findings_text = text
    confirmed_safe_idx = text.find("### Confirmed Safe")
    if confirmed_safe_idx >= 0:
        findings_text = text[:confirmed_safe_idx]

    # Parse individual findings
    finding_pattern = re.compile(
        r"####\s+\[([^\]]+)\]\s+(.+?)\s*\((\w+)\)\s*\n(.*?)(?=####|\Z)",
        re.DOTALL,
    )

    findings = []
    for match in finding_pattern.finditer(findings_text):
        bug_id = match.group(1)
        title_raw = match.group(2).strip()
        severity = match.group(3)
        body = match.group(4).strip()

        # Determine if this is a security finding
        is_security = "Security --" in title_raw or "Security —" in title_raw

        # Clean up title: remove category prefix like "Action Handler -- "
        title = re.sub(r"^.*?--\s*", "", title_raw).strip()
        # Get category
        category_match = re.match(r"^(.*?)\s*--", title_raw)
        category = category_match.group(1).strip() if category_match else ""

        findings.append({
            "bug_id": bug_id,
            "title": title,
            "category": category,
            "severity": severity,
            "body": body,
            "is_security": is_security,
        })

    return {
        "repo_name": repo_name,
        "total": total,
        "severity_summary": severity_summary,
        "areas": areas,
        "findings": findings,
    }


def make_github_link(location: str, github_base: str | None) -> str:
    """Convert a location like `src/charm.py:358` to a GitHub link."""
    if not github_base:
        return f"`{location}`"

    # Parse file:line
    loc_match = re.match(r"`?([^`:]+?)(?::(\d+))?`?$", location.strip())
    if not loc_match:
        return f"`{location}`"

    filepath = loc_match.group(1)
    line = loc_match.group(2)

    if line:
        # Could be multiple lines like "425,449,474,498"
        lines = line.split(",")
        first_line = lines[0].strip()
        return f"[`{filepath}:{line}`]({github_base}/{filepath}#L{first_line})"
    else:
        return f"[`{filepath}`]({github_base}/{filepath})"


def transform_finding_body(body: str, github_base: str | None) -> str:
    """Transform a finding body from internal format to writeup format."""
    lines = body.split("\n")
    output_lines = []
    skip_until_next_field = False

    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        line = lines[i]

        # Skip Pattern line
        if line.startswith("- **Pattern**:"):
            i += 1
            continue

        # Skip Historical precedent
        if line.startswith("- **Historical precedent**:"):
            i += 1
            continue

        # Transform Location to include GitHub link
        loc_match = re.match(r"- \*\*Location\*\*:\s*(.+)", line)
        if loc_match:
            location = loc_match.group(1).strip()
            # Handle multiple locations (e.g., "src/charm.py:425,449,474,498")
            linked = make_github_link(location, github_base)
            output_lines.append(f"**Location**: {linked}")
            output_lines.append("")
            i += 1
            continue

        # Transform field markers from "- **X**:" to "**X**:"
        field_match = re.match(r"- \*\*(\w[\w\s]*)\*\*:\s*(.*)", line)
        if field_match:
            field_name = field_match.group(1)
            field_value = field_match.group(2)

            # Rename Evidence -> Code
            if field_name == "Evidence":
                field_name = "Code"

            # Ensure blank line before each field for readability
            if output_lines and output_lines[-1] != "":
                output_lines.append("")
            output_lines.append(f"**{field_name}**: {field_value}")
            i += 1
            continue

        # Pass through everything else (code blocks, etc.)
        output_lines.append(line)
        i += 1

    # Clean up trailing whitespace and excessive blank lines
    result = "\n".join(output_lines).strip()
    result = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", result)
    return result


def is_pebble_env_finding(finding: dict) -> bool:
    """Check if a finding is about non-string Pebble environment values."""
    title_lower = finding["title"].lower()
    body_lower = finding["body"].lower()
    return (
        ("non-string" in title_lower or "non_string" in title_lower)
        and ("pebble" in title_lower or "environment" in title_lower)
    ) or (
        "ap-005" in body_lower
        and "pebble" in body_lower
    )


def get_pebble_env_caveat(finding: dict) -> str:
    """Return the appropriate Pebble env caveat for a finding, or empty string."""
    if not is_pebble_env_finding(finding):
        return ""

    body_lower = finding["body"].lower()

    has_none = "none" in body_lower and (
        "none value" in body_lower
        or "returns none" in body_lower
        or "return none" in body_lower
        or "is none" in body_lower
        or "be none" in body_lower
        or "can be `none`" in body_lower
        or "rejects `none`" in body_lower
        or "rejects none" in body_lower
        or "= none" in body_lower
    )
    has_bytes = "bytes" in body_lower or "b64encode" in body_lower

    if has_none:
        return PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_NONE
    elif has_bytes:
        return PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_BYTES
    else:
        return PEBBLE_ENV_CAVEAT_NON_STRING_ONLY


def generate_writeup(data: dict, github_base: str | None) -> str:
    """Generate the full writeup markdown for a repo."""
    # Filter out security findings
    non_security = [f for f in data["findings"] if not f["is_security"]]

    if not non_security:
        return ""

    # Recalculate counts
    severity_counts = {s: 0 for s in SEVERITY_ORDER}
    for f in non_security:
        if f["severity"] in severity_counts:
            severity_counts[f["severity"]] += 1

    total = sum(severity_counts.values())
    severity_parts = []
    for s in SEVERITY_ORDER:
        if severity_counts[s] > 0:
            severity_parts.append(f"{severity_counts[s]} {s}")
    severity_summary = ", ".join(severity_parts)

    output = TEMPLATE_INTRO.format(
        repo_name=data["repo_name"],
        total_repos=TOTAL_REPOS,
        total=total,
        severity_summary=severity_summary,
    )

    # Group findings by severity
    for severity in SEVERITY_ORDER:
        findings_at_level = [
            f for f in non_security if f["severity"] == severity
        ]
        if not findings_at_level:
            continue

        output += f"\n### {severity}\n\n"

        for finding in findings_at_level:
            output += f"#### {finding['bug_id']}: {finding['title']}\n\n"
            transformed = transform_finding_body(finding["body"], github_base)
            output += transformed
            # Add Pebble env caveat if applicable
            caveat = get_pebble_env_caveat(finding)
            if caveat:
                output += caveat
            output += "\n\n---\n\n"

    return output.rstrip("\n-— \n") + "\n"


def main():
    OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

    validation_files = sorted(DATA_DIR.glob("skill_validation_*.md"))
    print(f"Found {len(validation_files)} validation files")

    generated = 0
    skipped_no_findings = 0
    skipped_all_security = 0

    for vf in validation_files:
        data = parse_validation_file(vf)

        # Find the repo directory
        repo_dir = get_repo_dir_name(data["repo_name"])
        github_base = get_github_url(repo_dir) if repo_dir else None

        if not github_base:
            print(f"  WARNING: No GitHub URL for {data['repo_name']} (dir: {repo_dir})")

        non_security = [f for f in data["findings"] if not f["is_security"]]
        if not non_security:
            if data["findings"]:
                skipped_all_security += 1
                print(f"  SKIP (all security): {data['repo_name']}")
            else:
                skipped_no_findings += 1
                print(f"  SKIP (no findings): {data['repo_name']}")
            continue

        writeup = generate_writeup(data, github_base)
        if not writeup:
            skipped_no_findings += 1
            continue

        # Output filename: use the repo name, sanitised
        safe_name = re.sub(r"[^a-zA-Z0-9_-]", "_", data["repo_name"])
        output_path = OUTPUT_DIR / f"{safe_name}.md"
        output_path.write_text(writeup)
        generated += 1

        # Count findings
        sec_count = len(data["findings"]) - len(non_security)
        sec_note = f" ({sec_count} security excluded)" if sec_count else ""
        print(f"  OK: {data['repo_name']} -> {output_path.name} ({len(non_security)} findings{sec_note})")

    print(f"\nDone: {generated} writeups generated, {skipped_no_findings} skipped (no findings), {skipped_all_security} skipped (all security)")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

file.content:
Hi!

Hope you're doing well! Also apologies for disturbing you at a busy time of year.

I've been doing some experimentation into building a skill that finds bugs in charms, based on all the bugs that have already been fixed. You don't need to read all the gory details, but they are in the [Charming with Claude](https://github.com/tonyandrewmeyer/charming-with-claude) experiment "2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data" if you do.

After validating the skill against repos not used in its construction, I ran it across 42 repositories. I've attached the findings for your repos below. Each finding includes the location, a description of the issue, its impact, the relevant code, and a suggested fix.

I have not verified these findings against the running charm — they are based on static analysis of the source code at the time of review. Some may be mitigated by runtime conditions not visible in the code, or may have already been addressed.

I don't want to open a bunch of issues or PRs in your repos with what might turn out to be AI slop. On the other hand, if there are actual bugs here, it would be good to solve them. Also, somewhat selfishly, I would love extra data on any false positives to feed back into the skill, to try to make it something actually useful across charming.

Basically: the ball is in your court. You can ignore these, you can review them and open issues yourself (in many cases Copilot should be able to provide a draft fix; these aren't complex issues). You can ask me to open issues and/or PRs.

Your repos with findings:

- **kafka-k8s-operator** (2 findings)
- **opensearch-operator** (2 findings)
- **pgbouncer-k8s-operator** (6 findings)
- **pgbouncer-operator** (4 findings)
- **postgresql-k8s-operator** (6 findings)
- **postgresql-operator** (5 findings)
- **redis-k8s-operator** (5 findings)
- **zookeeper** (4 findings)

Ngā mihi,
Tony

content.content:
1→# Bug Findings: kafka-k8s-operator
     2→
     3→## Summary
     4→
     5→- **Findings**: 2 (1 Medium, 1 Low)
     6→
     7→Note that the severity was picked by AI and I don't necessarily agree with all of them, but it's a handy base classification.
     8→
     9→(If there's anything that looks to me (not just AI) like a security finding, they were excluded and I'll handle them separately.)
    10→
    11→## Findings
    12→
    13→### Medium
    14→
    15→#### BUG-001: `_on_peer_cluster_broken` handler defined but never registered
    16→
    17→**Location**: [`src/events/peer_cluster.py:182`](https://github.com/canonical/kafka-k8s-operator/blob/main/src/events/peer_cluster.py#L182)
    18→
    19→**Issue**: The `_on_peer_cluster_broken` method is defined and contains cleanup logic (stopping the workload, removing meta.properties, clearing credentials), but it is never registered with `self.framework.observe()`. The `__init__` method registers `relation_created` and `relation_changed` handlers for both `peer-cluster` and `peer-cluster-orchestrator` relations, but no `relation_broken` handler is ever wired up for either.
    20→
    21→**Impact**: When the `peer-cluster` or `peer-cluster-orchestrator` relation is removed, no cleanup occurs. The workload continues running with stale configuration, meta.properties files referencing the old cluster are not removed, and internal credentials are not cleared. Re-relating to a different controller/orchestrator cluster will fail because of conflicting ClusterID in meta.properties.
    22→
    23→**Code**: 
    24→  ```python
    25→  # In __init__: handlers registered for created and changed, but NOT broken
    26→  for relation_name in [PEER_CLUSTER_RELATION, PEER_CLUSTER_ORCHESTRATOR_RELATION]:
    27→      self.framework.observe(
    28→          self.charm.on[relation_name].relation_created,
    29→          self._on_peer_cluster_created,
    30→      )
    31→
    32→  # ... no observe for relation_broken ...
    33→
    34→  # Method exists but is dead code:
    35→  def _on_peer_cluster_broken(self, _: RelationBrokenEvent):
    36→      """Handle the required logic to remove."""
    37→      if self.charm.state.runs_controller:
    38→          return
    39→      self.charm.workload.stop()
    40→      # ...
    41→  ```
    42→
    43→**Recommended fix**: 
    44→  ```python
    45→  # Add to __init__, after the relation_created observers:
    46→  for relation_name in [PEER_CLUSTER_RELATION, PEER_CLUSTER_ORCHESTRATOR_RELATION]:
    47→      self.framework.observe(
    48→          self.charm.on[relation_name].relation_broken,
    49→          self._on_peer_cluster_broken,
    50→      )
    51→  ```
    52→
    53→---
    54→
    55→
    56→### Low
    57→
    58→#### BUG-002: Typo in BALANCER `requested_secrets`
    59→
    60→**Location**: [`src/literals.py:183`](https://github.com/canonical/kafka-k8s-operator/blob/main/src/literals.py#L183)
    61→
    62→**Issue**: The `BALANCER` role's `requested_secrets` list contains `"controller-passwrod"` (typo) instead of `"controller-password"`. This means the balancer role will never successfully request the controller password secret from the peer-cluster relation, as the key name doesn't match what the providing side sets.
    63→
    64→**Impact**: In a multi-app deployment where the balancer runs in a separate application from the controller, the balancer will fail to receive the controller password through the secrets mechanism. The impact is mitigated because in single-app mode (roles=broker,balancer,controller), the password is available through the local peer data. In multi-app mode, the `controller-password` is typically provided through a different code path. However, the typo indicates the secret request for this field silently fails.
    65→
    66→**Code**: 
    67→  ```python
    68→  BALANCER = Role(
    69→      value="balancer",
    70→      service="cruise-control",
    71→      paths=PATHS["cruise-control"],
    72→      relation=PEER_CLUSTER_RELATION,

file.content:
# Bug Findings: kafka-k8s-operator

## Summary

- **Findings**: 2 (1 Medium, 1 Low)

Note that the severity was picked by AI and I don't necessarily agree with all of them, but it's a handy base classification.

(If there's anything that looks to me (not just AI) like a security finding, they were excluded and I'll handle them separately.)

## Findings

### Medium

#### BUG-001: `_on_peer_cluster_broken` handler defined but never registered

**Location**: [`src/events/peer_cluster.py:182`](https://github.com/canonical/kafka-k8s-operator/blob/main/src/events/peer_cluster.py#L182)

**Issue**: The `_on_peer_cluster_broken` method is defined and contains cleanup logic (stopping the workload, removing meta.properties, clearing credentials), but it is never registered with `self.framework.observe()`. The `__init__` method registers `relation_created` and `relation_changed` handlers for both `peer-cluster` and `peer-cluster-orchestrator` relations, but no `relation_broken` handler is ever wired up for either.

**Impact**: When the `peer-cluster` or `peer-cluster-orchestrator` relation is removed, no cleanup occurs. The workload continues running with stale configuration, meta.properties files referencing the old cluster are not removed, and internal credentials are not cleared. Re-relating to a different controller/orchestrator cluster will fail because of conflicting ClusterID in meta.properties.

**Code**: 
  ```python
  # In __init__: handlers registered for created and changed, but NOT broken
  for relation_name in [PEER_CLUSTER_RELATION, PEER_CLUSTER_ORCHESTRATOR_RELATION]:
      self.framework.observe(
          self.charm.on[relation_name].relation_created,
          self._on_peer_cluster_created,
      )

  # ... no observe for relation_broken ...

  # Method exists but is dead code:
  def _on_peer_cluster_broken(self, _: RelationBrokenEvent):
      """Handle the required logic to remove."""
      if self.charm.state.runs_controller:
          return
      self.charm.workload.stop()
      # ...
  ```

**Recommended fix**: 
  ```python
  # Add to __init__, after the relation_created observers:
  for relation_name in [PEER_CLUSTER_RELATION, PEER_CLUSTER_ORCHESTRATOR_RELATION]:
      self.framework.observe(
          self.charm.on[relation_name].relation_broken,
          self._on_peer_cluster_broken,
      )
  ```

---


### Low

#### BUG-002: Typo in BALANCER `requested_secrets`

**Location**: [`src/literals.py:183`](https://github.com/canonical/kafka-k8s-operator/blob/main/src/literals.py#L183)

**Issue**: The `BALANCER` role's `requested_secrets` list contains `"controller-passwrod"` (typo) instead of `"controller-password"`. This means the balancer role will never successfully request the controller password secret from the peer-cluster relation, as the key name doesn't match what the providing side sets.

**Impact**: In a multi-app deployment where the balancer runs in a separate application from the controller, the balancer will fail to receive the controller password through the secrets mechanism. The impact is mitigated because in single-app mode (roles=broker,balancer,controller), the password is available through the local peer data. In multi-app mode, the `controller-password` is typically provided through a different code path. However, the typo indicates the secret request for this field silently fails.

**Code**: 
  ```python
  BALANCER = Role(
      value="balancer",
      service="cruise-control",
      paths=PATHS["cruise-control"],
      relation=PEER_CLUSTER_RELATION,
      requested_secrets=[
          "broker-username",
          "broker-password",
          "broker-uris",
          "controller-passwrod",  # BUG: typo, should be "controller-password"
          "zk-username",
          "zk-password",
          "zk-uris",
      ],
  )
  ```

**Recommended fix**: 
  ```python
  requested_secrets=[
      "broker-username",
      "broker-password",
      "broker-uris",
      "controller-password",  # Fixed typo
      "zk-username",
      "zk-password",
      "zk-uris",
  ],
  ```

    33→data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-61-- **Issue**: Inside `list_cas()`, for each certificate block parsed from the PKCS12 truststore, the code searches for a line starting with `friendlyName:` using `next(..., None)`. If no such line exists (e.g., a certificate block lacks a friendlyName attribute, which can happen with certain PKCS12 formats or malformed stores), `alias_line` is `None`, and the subsequent `alias_line.split("friendlyName:", 1)` raises `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'split'`.
    34→data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-62-- **Impact**: Unhandled `AttributeError` crashes the charm. This function is called by `read_ca()` (used extensively in TLS certificate management) and by the snapshots manager. Any corruption or unexpected format in the PKCS12 store would crash the charm on every event until the store is manually repaired.

    42→data/skill_validation_opensearch.md:84:#### [BUG-004] Security -- Hardcoded truststore password "changeit" for backup PKCS12 store (Medium)
    43→data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-85-- **Location**: `lib/charms/opensearch/v0/opensearch_snapshots.py:100`
    44→data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-86-- **Pattern**: AP-019 (World-readable credential files variant -- hardcoded credential)
    45→data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-87-- **Issue**: The PKCS12 truststore used for S3/Azure/GCS backup CA certificates uses the hardcoded password `STORE_PASSWORD = "changeit"`. This is the well-known default Java keystore password. While this store only holds CA certificates (not private keys), the password is used in multiple `keytool` and `openssl` CLI commands via `run_cmd()`, making it visible in process listing, and any user on the system who knows this password can modify the truststore to inject rogue CA certificates.
    46→data/skill_validation_opensearch.md-88-- **Impact**: A local attacker could modify the backup truststore to inject a malicious CA, enabling MITM attacks on backup traffic to S3/Azure/GCS endpoints. The impact is somewhat reduced because: (1) only CA certs are stored (not private keys), and (2) the snap_daemon user has restricted file access. However, the password is trivially guessable and used in CLI arguments visible via `ps`.

1→## Bug Review: mlflow-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 4 (0 Critical, 1 High, 3 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble container management, relation data handling, status management, credential exposure, configuration handling, ingress/networking, error handling
     6→- **Charm name**: mlflow-server
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Pebble -- Wrong container used for plan comparison in _update_layer (High)
    11→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:498`
    12→- **Pattern**: Novel
    13→- **Issue**: The `_update_layer` method accepts a `container` parameter but always reads the current plan from `self.container` (the mlflow-server container) instead of the passed `container` argument. When this method is called for the exporter container (lines 587-591), it compares the mlflow-server plan against the exporter layer.
    14→- **Impact**: When called for the exporter container, the plan comparison is between the wrong containers. If the mlflow-server plan happens to match the exporter layer's services (unlikely but possible), the exporter layer update will be silently skipped. In the common case, the comparison will always show a difference, causing unnecessary replans of the exporter container on every event even when nothing has changed.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  def _update_layer(self, container, container_name, new_layer) -> None:
    18→      current_layer = self.container.get_plan()  # BUG: should be `container.get_plan()`
    19→      if current_layer.services != new_layer.services:
    20→          self.unit.status = MaintenanceStatus("Applying new pebble layer")
    21→          container.add_layer(container_name, new_layer, combine=True)
    22→  ```
    23→- **Recommended fix**:
    24→  ```python
    25→  def _update_layer(self, container, container_name, new_layer) -> None:
    26→      current_layer = container.get_plan()
    27→      if current_layer.services != new_layer.services:
    28→          self.unit.status = MaintenanceStatus("Applying new pebble layer")
    29→          container.add_layer(container_name, new_layer, combine=True)
    30→  ```
    31→- **Historical precedent**: Common pattern where methods accept a parameter but reference an instance attribute of the same concept. Similar to container name assumption bugs found in multiple charms.
    32→
    33→#### [BUG-002] Security -- Database credentials logged in debug output (Medium)
    34→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:367`
    35→- **Pattern**: AP-018 (Credential logging)
    36→- **Issue**: The `_get_relational_db_data` method logs the entire result of `fetch_relation_data()` at debug level. This dict contains `username` and `password` fields from the database relation.
    37→- **Impact**: Database credentials appear in plain text in Juju debug-log output. Anyone with access to `juju debug-log` can read the database password.
    38→- **Evidence**:
    39→  ```python
    40→  data = self.database.fetch_relation_data()
    41→  self.logger.debug("Got following database data: %s", data)
    42→  ```
    43→- **Recommended fix**:
    44→  ```python
    45→  data = self.database.fetch_relation_data()
    46→  self.logger.debug("Got database data for relation IDs: %s", list(data.keys()))
    47→  ```
    48→- **Historical precedent**: Credential logging is one of the most common security bugs across charm repositories, found in 45+ repos.
    49→
    50→#### [BUG-003] Security -- Database password exposed in Pebble service command string (Medium)

1→## Bug Review: opensearch-operator (VM charm) -- src/ and lib/charms/opensearch/
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 4 (0 Critical, 3 High, 1 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: TLS/Security, Upgrade, Service management, Backup/Snapshots, Configuration
     6→- **Charm name**: opensearch-operator
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Configuration -- Incorrect /proc/pid/stat parsing causes wrong service status detection (High)
    11→- **Location**: `src/opensearch.py:112-117`
    12→- **Pattern**: novel
    13→- **Issue**: The code reads `/proc/{pid}/stat` and checks `stat[2]` (the 3rd character of the raw string) to determine process state. However, the `/proc/pid/stat` format is `pid (comm) state ppid ...`. For any PID with more than one digit (which is virtually all processes), `stat[2]` indexes into the PID number itself, not the state field. For example, with PID `12345`, the string starts with `12345 (java) S ...`, and `stat[2]` is `'3'` (the digit 3), not `'S'` (sleeping).
    14→- **Impact**: The function `is_service_started()` returns incorrect results. Because `'3'` (or any digit) is not in `["Z", "T", "X"]`, the function incorrectly returns `True` for any non-zombie process, but this is accidental. For the `paused=True` path (`stat[2] == "T"`), paused detection never works for multi-digit PIDs because `stat[2]` is always a digit from the PID.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  with open(f"/proc/{pid}/stat") as f:
    18→      stat = f.read()
    19→  # ...
    20→  if stat[2] == "T" and paused:
    21→      return True
    22→  return stat[2] not in ["Z", "T", "X"]
    23→  ```
    24→- **Recommended fix**:
    25→  ```python
    26→  with open(f"/proc/{pid}/stat") as f:
    27→      stat = f.read()
    28→  # The state is the 3rd field, after "pid (comm) ".
    29→  # comm can contain spaces/parens, so find the last ')' first.
    30→  state = stat[stat.rfind(")") + 2]
    31→  if state == "T" and paused:
    32→      return True
    33→  return state not in ["Z", "T", "X"]
    34→  ```
    35→- **Historical precedent**: Novel -- proc filesystem parsing bugs are uncommon in charms but common in system monitoring code.
    36→
    37→---
    38→
    39→#### [BUG-002] Configuration -- Literal `$` in SNAP_LOG_DIR environment variable (High)
    40→- **Location**: `src/opensearch.py:171`
    41→- **Pattern**: AP-002 (Missing f-string prefix variant -- shell/Python syntax confusion)
    42→- **Issue**: The environment variable `SNAP_LOG_DIR` is set to `f"${self._SNAP_COMMON}/ops/snap/logs"`. Because `$` is a literal character in Python f-strings (only `{...}` triggers interpolation), the `$` is preserved verbatim. The resulting value is `$/var/snap/opensearch/common/ops/snap/logs` -- a path starting with a literal `$` character. The other env vars on adjacent lines correctly use `f"{self._SNAP}/..."` and `f"{self.paths.home}/..."` without the `$`.
    43→- **Impact**: Any code or process that reads `SNAP_LOG_DIR` gets an invalid path starting with `$`. If used for log file creation, logs would be written to a non-existent directory (or a directory literally named `$var`), causing silent log loss or errors.
    44→- **Evidence**:
    45→  ```python
    46→  os.environ["SNAP_LOG_DIR"] = f"${self._SNAP_COMMON}/ops/snap/logs"  # BUG: literal $
    47→  os.environ["OPS_ROOT"] = f"{self._SNAP}/opt/opensearch"             # correct
    48→  os.environ["OPENSEARCH_BIN"] = f"{self._SNAP}/usr/share/opensearch/bin"  # correct
    49→  ```

1→## Bug Review: openfga-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 3 (0 Critical, 1 High, 2 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, relations and events, TLS/certificates, configuration handling, status management, ingress/networking, credential handling, action handlers, error handling, URL construction
     6→- **Charm name**: openfga-k8s
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Pebble -- Non-string boolean values in environment dicts (Medium)
    11→- **Location**: `src/env_vars.py:9` and `src/integrations.py:298`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-005 (None or non-string values in environment dicts)
    13→- **Issue**: Python `bool` values are used directly in Pebble service environment dictionaries. `False` on line 9 of `env_vars.py` and `True` on line 298 of `integrations.py` are Python bools, not strings. When serialised to Pebble, Python `True` becomes `"True"` (capital T) and `False` becomes `"False"` (capital F).
    14→- **Impact**: The OpenFGA Go binary receives `OPENFGA_PLAYGROUND_ENABLED=False` and `OPENFGA_TRACE_ENABLED=True` with capital first letters. While Go's `strconv.ParseBool` accepts these, other parsing mechanisms in the application may not. More importantly, Pebble's documented API expects string values for environment variables; passing non-strings relies on implicit type coercion that may break across Pebble versions.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  # env_vars.py:9
    18→  "OPENFGA_PLAYGROUND_ENABLED": False,
    19→
    20→  # integrations.py:298
    21→  "OPENFGA_TRACE_ENABLED": True,
    22→  ```
    23→- **Recommended fix**:
    24→  ```python
    25→  # env_vars.py:9
    26→  "OPENFGA_PLAYGROUND_ENABLED": "false",
    27→
    28→  # integrations.py:298
    29→  "OPENFGA_TRACE_ENABLED": "true",
    30→  ```
    31→- **Historical precedent**: hydra-operator had the same issue with Python bools in Pebble environment dicts.
    32→
    33→#### [BUG-002] Ingress -- Stale URI scheme captured at init time (High)
    34→- **Location**: `src/integrations.py:319`
    35→- **Pattern**: Novel (init-time snapshot of dynamic state)
    36→- **Issue**: `HttpIngressIntegration.__init__` captures `self._uri_scheme = charm._certs_integration.uri_scheme` once at charm initialisation. Since `uri_scheme` depends on whether TLS certificate files exist in the container, and certificates are pushed later during event handling via `_holistic_handler` -> `update_certificates()`, the captured scheme is always `"http"` when the charm initialises -- even if TLS certificates will be pushed during the same event.
    37→- **Impact**: When ingress is not ready, the fallback URL advertised to relation consumers via `openfga_provider.update_relations_app_data` uses `http://` instead of `https://`, even when TLS is fully configured. Consumers connecting to this URL either fail TLS handshake or connect insecurely.
    38→- **Evidence**:
    39→  ```python
    40→  # integrations.py:319 -- captured once at init
    41→  self._uri_scheme = charm._certs_integration.uri_scheme
    42→
    43→  # integrations.py:328-333 -- used in url property with stale scheme
    44→  @property
    45→  def url(self) -> str:
    46→      k8s_svc = (
    47→          f"{self._uri_scheme}://{self._charm.app.name}.{self._charm.model.name}.svc.cluster.local"
    48→          f":{OPENFGA_SERVER_HTTP_PORT}"
    49→      )
    50→      return self.ingress_requirer.url if self.ingress_requirer.is_ready() else k8s_svc

1→## Bug Review: identity-platform-admin-ui-operator

     4→- **Findings**: 5 (0 Critical, 2 High, 3 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, relation data, TLS/certificates, configuration handling, status management, URL construction, credential handling, action handlers, environment variables
     6→- **Charm name**: identity-platform-admin-ui
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Pebble -- Non-string values in Pebble environment dicts (High)
    11→
    12→- **Location**: `src/env_vars.py:11-12`, `src/env_vars.py:24`, `src/env_vars.py:34`, `src/env_vars.py:40`, `src/integrations.py:265`, `src/integrations.py:315`, `src/configs.py:19`
    13→- **Pattern**: AP-005 (None or non-string values in environment dicts)
    14→- **Issue**: Multiple Python `bool` and `int` values are placed into Pebble service environment dictionaries without conversion to strings. `DEFAULT_CONTAINER_ENV` contains `False` (lines 11, 34, 40), `True` (line 12), and `1025` (line 24, an `int`). Several `to_env_vars()` methods also return `bool` values: `TracingData.to_env_vars()` returns `self.is_ready` (a `bool`) for `TRACING_ENABLED`, `OAuthProviderData.to_env_vars()` returns `self.auth_enabled` (a `bool`) for `AUTHENTICATION_ENABLED`, and `CharmConfig.to_env_vars()` returns a `bool` for `DEBUG`. The `EnvVars` type alias even codifies this: `Mapping[str, Union[str, bool]]`. All these flow into `render_pebble_layer` and into the Pebble Layer.
    15→- **Impact**: Python `bool` values become `"True"` / `"False"` (capital T/F) when serialised. Go-based workloads typically expect lowercase `"true"` / `"false"`. The `int` value `1025` becomes `"1025"` which happens to be correct, but `AUTHORIZATION_ENABLED: True` becomes `"True"` rather than `"true"`, which may cause the workload to treat it as an unrecognised value, silently disabling authorisation.

    17→  ```python
    18→  # env_vars.py
    19→  DEFAULT_CONTAINER_ENV = {

    29→
    30→  # integrations.py:263-265
    31→  def to_env_vars(self) -> EnvVars:
    32→      return {
    33→          "TRACING_ENABLED": self.is_ready,  # bool
    34→
    35→  # configs.py:17-19
    36→  return {
    37→      "LOG_LEVEL": log_level,
    38→      "DEBUG": "DEBUG" == log_level,  # bool
    39→  }
    40→  ```
    41→- **Recommended fix**:
    42→  ```python
    43→  # Use lowercase string booleans throughout
    44→  "AUTHENTICATION_ENABLED": "false",
    45→  "AUTHORIZATION_ENABLED": "true",
    46→  "TRACING_ENABLED": "false",
    47→  "DEBUG": "false",
    48→  "MAIL_PORT": "1025",
    49→
    50→  # In to_env_vars methods, convert:

1→## Bug Review: wordpress-k8s-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 5 (1 Critical, 1 High, 2 Medium, 1 Low)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/containers, relation data, configuration handling, status management, URL construction, security/credentials, action handlers, Grafana dashboards
     6→- **Charm name**: wordpress-k8s
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] URL Construction -- os.path.join used to build URL (Medium)
    11→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:1482`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-057
    13→- **Issue**: `os.path.join()` is used to construct a URL from `swift_url`, `bucket`, and `object_prefix`. If `bucket` or `object_prefix` starts with `/`, `os.path.join` will discard all preceding path components. For example, `os.path.join("http://host:8080/v1/AUTH_abc", "/WordPress", "wp-content/uploads/")` returns `/WordPress/wp-content/uploads/`, discarding the entire swift URL.
    14→- **Impact**: The Apache ProxyPass directive receives a broken `redirect_url`, causing all upload proxying to fail silently or route to the wrong destination. This only triggers when the OpenStack Swift plugin is configured with a `bucket` or `object-prefix` value starting with `/`.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  redirect_url = os.path.join(swift_url, bucket, object_prefix)
    18→  conf = textwrap.dedent(
    19→      f"""\
    20→  SSLProxyEngine on
    21→  ProxyPass /wp-content/uploads/ {redirect_url}
    22→  ProxyPassReverse /wp-content/uploads/ {redirect_url}
    23→  Timeout 300
    24→  """
    25→  )
    26→  ```
    27→- **Recommended fix**:
    28→  ```python
    29→  redirect_url = f"{swift_url.rstrip('/')}/{bucket.strip('/')}/{object_prefix.strip('/')}"
    30→  ```
    31→- **Historical precedent**: kratos-operator used `os.path.join` for OIDC callback URL construction with the same class of bug.
    32→
    33→#### [BUG-002] Security -- Unescaped DB password interpolated into PHP code (Critical)
    34→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:453`
    35→- **Pattern**: novel
    36→- **Issue**: The database password from the MySQL relation is interpolated directly into a PHP `define()` statement using an f-string without escaping single quotes. If the password contains a single quote character (`'`), the generated `wp-config.php` will have a PHP syntax error, preventing WordPress from starting. In a worst-case scenario, a password containing `'); <PHP code> //` would result in arbitrary PHP code execution.
    37→- **Impact**: A database password containing single quotes breaks WordPress entirely. While the MySQL charm typically generates safe passwords, any change in the database provider's password generation logic (or manual password setting) could trigger this. The same pattern applies to `DB_HOST`, `DB_NAME`, and `DB_USER` (lines 448-451), and to the WordPress secret keys (line 460).
    38→- **Evidence**:
    39→  ```python
    40→  wp_config.append(
    41→      f"define( 'DB_PASSWORD', '{self._current_effective_db_info.password}' );"
    42→  )
    43→  ```
    44→- **Recommended fix**:
    45→  ```python
    46→  escaped_password = self._current_effective_db_info.password.replace("\\", "\\\\").replace("'", "\\'")
    47→  wp_config.append(
    48→      f"define( 'DB_PASSWORD', '{escaped_password}' );"
    49→  )
    50→  ```

    46→#### [BUG-002] Relations -- get_secret(id=None) crashes when secret-id not yet set (High)

1→## Bug Review: pgbouncer-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 5 (1 Critical, 2 High, 2 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Relations and Events, TLS and Security, Configuration Handling, Status Management, Database Interaction, Juju Interaction, Error Handling, Security
     6→- **Charm name**: pgbouncer
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Relations -- Missing None guard on version field before calling split (High)
    11→- **Location**: `src/relations/backend_database.py:438-441`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-064
    13→- **Issue**: The `postgres` property fetches `version` from relation data on line 435 but omits it from the None check on line 438. Line 441 then calls `version.split(".")` unconditionally, which raises `AttributeError` when `version` is None.
    14→- **Impact**: During early relation setup, the `version` field may not yet be populated in the relation databag. Any code path that accesses `self.backend.postgres` (which includes almost every event handler) will crash the charm with an unhandled `AttributeError`, preventing PgBouncer from configuring itself until the PostgreSQL charm sets the version field.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  endpoint = databag.get("endpoints")
    18→  user = self.database.fetch_relation_field(self.relation.id, "username")
    19→  password = self.database.fetch_relation_field(self.relation.id, "password")
    20→  version = self.database.fetch_relation_field(self.relation.id, "version")
    21→  database = self.database.database
    22→
    23→  if None in [endpoint, user, password]:
    24→      return None
    25→
    26→  if version.split(".")[0] == "14":  # Crashes if version is None
    27→  ```
    28→- **Recommended fix**:
    29→  ```python
    30→  if None in [endpoint, user, password, version]:
    31→      return None
    32→  ```
    33→- **Historical precedent**: This is the exact pattern documented as AP-064, observed in other charms. The postgresql-k8s-operator had a similar bug with version field access.
    34→
    35→#### [BUG-002] Database -- String comparison of version numbers in sync_hba (High)
    36→- **Location**: `src/relations/backend_database.py:576`
    37→- **Pattern**: AP-054
    38→- **Issue**: `self.backend_version < "14.16"` performs lexicographic string comparison instead of semantic version comparison. For example, `"14.2" < "14.16"` evaluates to `False` lexicographically (because `"2" > "1"`), but 14.2 is semantically less than 14.16.
    39→- **Impact**: On PostgreSQL versions 14.2 through 14.9 (all of which lack the HBA hardening feature), the guard fails to return early, causing `sync_hba` to call `is_user_in_hba()` on a backend that does not support it. This could raise an unexpected error or cause incorrect behaviour during user setup on `db`, `db-admin`, and `database` relation joins.
    40→- **Evidence**:
    41→  ```python
    42→  if self.backend_version and self.backend_version < "14.16":
    43→      return
    44→  ```
    45→- **Recommended fix**:
    46→  ```python
    47→  if self.backend_version:
    48→      major, minor = self.backend_version.split(".")
    49→      if (int(major), int(minor)) < (14, 16):
    50→          return

    13→- **Issue**: `self.backend_version < "14.16"` performs lexicographic string comparison. The `backend_version` property returns a string (e.g., `"14.9"`). 1→## Bug Review: oathkeeper-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 4 (0 Critical, 2 High, 2 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, relation data handling, TLS/certificate lifecycle, status management, config handling, auth/identity, action handlers
     6→- **Charm name**: oathkeeper
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Pebble -- Non-string bytes values in Pebble environment dict for TLS env vars (High)
    11→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:236-237`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-005
    13→- **Issue**: `b64encode()` returns `bytes`, not `str`. The Pebble environment dict values `SERVE_API_TLS_CERT_BASE64` and `SERVE_API_TLS_KEY_BASE64` are `bytes` objects (e.g., `b'dGVzdA=='`), not strings. Pebble expects all environment values to be strings and will reject or mishandle `bytes` objects.
    14→- **Impact**: When TLS certificates are available, the Pebble layer will contain `bytes` values in the environment dict. This causes Pebble to fail to apply the layer or to produce incorrect environment variable values, breaking TLS configuration for the Oathkeeper workload.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  extra_env.update({
    18→      "SERVE_API_TLS_CERT_BASE64": b64encode(bytes(self.cert_handler.cert, "utf-8")),
    19→      "SERVE_API_TLS_KEY_BASE64": b64encode(bytes(self.cert_handler.key, "utf-8")),
    20→  })
    21→  ```
    22→- **Recommended fix**:
    23→  ```python
    24→  extra_env.update({
    25→      "SERVE_API_TLS_CERT_BASE64": b64encode(bytes(self.cert_handler.cert, "utf-8")).decode("ascii"),
    26→      "SERVE_API_TLS_KEY_BASE64": b64encode(bytes(self.cert_handler.key, "utf-8")).decode("ascii"),
    27→  })
    28→  ```
    29→- **Historical precedent**: Similar `bytes` vs `str` issues found in hydra-operator and SD-Core charms.
    30→
    31→#### [BUG-002] Pebble -- Non-string values (bool, int) in Pebble environment dict for tracing env vars (High)
    32→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:243-249`
    33→- **Pattern**: AP-005
    34→- **Issue**: When tracing is enabled, several environment values are Python `True` (bool) and `1` (int) instead of strings. `True` becomes `"True"` (capital T) when stringified, but Go/Oathkeeper expects lowercase `"true"`. The integer `1` is also not a string.
    35→- **Impact**: Pebble may reject the layer, or the environment variables will contain incorrect values (e.g., `"True"` instead of `"true"` for `TRACING_ENABLED`), causing Oathkeeper to fail to initialise tracing or to misparse the configuration.
    36→- **Evidence**:
    37→  ```python
    38→  extra_env.update({
    39→      "TRACING_ENABLED": True,
    40→      "TRACING_PROVIDER": "otel",
    41→      "TRACING_PROVIDERS_OTLP_SERVER_URL": self._get_tracing_endpoint_info(),
    42→      "TRACING_PROVIDERS_OTLP_INSECURE": True,
    43→      "TRACING_PROVIDERS_OTLP_SAMPLING_SAMPLING_RATIO": 1,
    44→  })
    45→  ```

    11→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:234-238`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-025
    13→- **Issue**: The charm logs config changes including plaintext passwords and keystore credentials. When `server_password`, `client_password`, `keystore_password`, and `truststore_password` are updated, the full previous values are logged via the config change handler and potentially appear in Juju logs.
    14→- **Impact**: Sensitive credentials leak into application logs which may be accessible to other applications, log aggregation systems, or operators who shouldn't have access. Security breach.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  def _on_config_changed(self, _: ConfigChangedEvent) -> None:
    18→      """Log config changes."""
    19→      logger.info(f"Config changed from {self._previous_config} to {self.charm.config}")

    21→  ```
    22→- **Recommended fix**:
    23→  ```python
    24→  def _on_config_changed(self, _: ConfigChangedEvent) -> None:
    25→      """Log config changes."""

    27→      safe_keys = [k for k in self.charm.config if k not in ['server-password', 'client-password', 'keystore-password', 'truststore-password']]
    28→      logger.info(f"Non-sensitive config changed: {safe_keys}")
    29→  ```

    31→
    32→#### [BUG-002] Error Handling -- UnboundLocalError in get_node_ip on non-403 ApiError (High)
    33→- **Location**: `src/charm.py:156-175`
    34→- **Pattern**: AP-042
    35→- **Issue**: `get_node_ip()` catches `ApiError` and checks `if e.status.code == 403`, but only initialises the `ip` variable inside the `403` branch. If an `ApiError` with a different status code occurs, the code tries to return `ip` which is not defined, raising `UnboundLocalError`.
    36→- **Impact**: The action handler crashes with `UnboundLocalError: local variable 'ip' referenced before assignment` on non-403 API errors, instead of failing gracefully.
    37→- **Evidence**:
    38→  ```python
    39→  def get_node_ip(self, node_id: str) -> str | None:
    40→      try:
    41→          ...
    42→      except ApiError as e:
    43→          if e.status.code == 403:
    44→              ip = None  # Only defined here
    45→          logger.error(f"Failed to get node IP: {e}")
    46→      return ip  # May be undefined if e.status.code != 403
    47→  ```

1→## Bug Review: synapse-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 6 (1 Critical, 2 High, 3 Medium)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Pebble/container management, relation data, status management, actions/error handling, configuration handling, Grafana dashboards, security/credential exposure, URL construction
     6→- **Charm name**: synapse
     7→
     8→### Findings
     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Actions -- Missing return after event.fail in anonymize_user_action (High)
    11→- **Location**: `src/actions.py:86`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-044
    13→- **Issue**: When `deactivate_user()` raises `MASDeactivateUserFailedError`, `event.fail()` is called but execution falls through to `event.set_results()` on line 87, reporting success.
    14→- **Impact**: A failed user deactivation is reported as successful to the operator. The action shows conflicting fail + results state.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  try:
    18→      deactivate_user(container=container, username=event.params["username"])
    19→  except MASDeactivateUserFailedError as exc:
    20→      logger.exception("Error deactivating user.")
    21→      event.fail(str(exc))
    22→
    23→  event.set_results(
    24→      {
    25→          "anonymize-user": True,
    26→      }
    27→  )
    28→  ```
    29→- **Recommended fix**:
    30→  ```python
    31→  try:
    32→      deactivate_user(container=container, username=event.params["username"])
    33→  except MASDeactivateUserFailedError as exc:
    34→      logger.exception("Error deactivating user.")
    35→      event.fail(str(exc))
    36→      return
    37→
    38→  event.set_results(
    39→      {
    40→          "anonymize-user": True,
    41→      }
    42→  )
    43→  ```
    44→- **Historical precedent**: hydra-operator had the same pattern in action handlers.
    45→
    46→#### [BUG-002] Pebble -- exec without wait and broken glob in registration secrets cleanup (High)
    47→- **Location**: `src/synapse/workload.py:366`
    48→- **Pattern**: AP-048
    49→- **Issue**: `container.exec(["rm", "-f", f"{SYNAPSE_CONFIG_DIR}/appservice-registration-*.yaml"])` has two problems: the return value is discarded without calling `.wait()`, so the command runs asynchronously and races with subsequent file writes; and Pebble's `exec()` does not invoke a shell, so the `*` glob character is passed as a literal filename argument to `rm` and no files are actually deleted.
    50→- **Impact**: Old registration secret files from removed matrix-auth relations are never cleaned up, causing stale application service registrations to persist in Synapse's configuration.

1→## Bug Review: hardware-observer-operator
     2→
     3→### Summary
     4→- **Findings**: 4 (0 Critical, 2 High, 1 Medium, 1 Low)
     5→- **Code areas reviewed**: Configuration handling, status management, credential handling, observability dashboards, action handlers, subprocess calls, error handling, data flow
     6→- **Charm name**: hardware-observer (subordinate machine charm)

     9→
    10→#### [BUG-001] Configuration -- Inverted boolean in redfish warning validator (High)
    11→- **Location**: `src/literals.py:78`
    12→- **Pattern**: AP-051 (Inverted boolean condition)
    13→- **Issue**: The validator fires the "experimental" warning when `redfish_disable is True` -- i.e. when Redfish is *disabled* (the default). The intent, per commit 137cf48 ("Warn users when redfish is enabled"), was to warn when the user *enables* Redfish by setting `redfish-disable` to `False`.
    14→- **Impact**: Every single charm initialisation logs a spurious warning about experimental Redfish features, even though Redfish is disabled. When the user actually enables Redfish (the scenario that should trigger the warning), no warning is produced.
    15→- **Evidence**:
    16→  ```python
    17→  if value is True:  # Fires when Redfish is DISABLED (the default!)
    18→      logger.warning(
    19→          "Redfish alert rules are considered experimental and may be changed or removed "
    20→          "in future releases."
    21→      )
    22→  ```
    23→- **Recommended fix**:
    24→  ```python
    25→  if value is False:  # Fires when Redfish is ENABLED
    26→      logger.warning(
    27→          "Redfish alert rules are considered experimental and may be changed or removed "
    28→          "in future releases."
    29→      )
    30→  ```
    31→- **Historical precedent**: vault-k8s-operator had an identical inverted `skip_verify is False` condition for S3 SSL verification.
    32→

    34→- **Location**: `src/hardware.py:118-128`
    35→- **Pattern**: AP-065 (Implicit None return used as valid value)
    36→- **Issue**: `get_nvidia_driver_version()` declares `-> int` but returns `None` implicitly when the regex does not match the NVIDIA version string (i.e. the file exists but has unexpected content). The caller `get_cuda_version_from_driver()` at line 133 then does `if driver_version >= 580:` which raises `TypeError: '>=' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'int'`.
    37→- **Impact**: If `/proc/driver/nvidia/version` exists but contains an unexpected format, the charm crashes with an unhandled `TypeError` instead of a descriptive error. This crashes the hook and puts the unit into error state.
    38→- **Evidence**:
    39→  ```python
    40→  def get_nvidia_driver_version() -> int:
    41→      try:
    42→          nvidia_driver_version = NVIDIA_DRIVER_PATH.read_text()

    44→          if match:
    45→              return int(match.group(1).split(".")[0])
    46→      except FileNotFoundError as e:
    47→          ...
    48→      # Implicit return None when match is None
    49→  ```

**Finding 1: wordpress-k8s-operator BUG-002** (Critical)
- Validation file: `skill_validation_wordpress.md`
- Unescaped DB password interpolated into PHP code at `src/charm.py:453`
- Branch name: `security/escape-db-password-in-wp-config`
- The fix: escape single quotes and backslashes in the password before interpolating into the PHP define() statement. Also escape DB_HOST, DB_NAME, DB_USER similarly.

**Finding 2: wordpress-k8s-operator BUG-003** (High)
- Validation file: `skill_validation_wordpress.md`

**Finding 3: oathkeeper-operator BUG-004** (Medium)
- Validation file: `skill_validation_oathkeeper.md`
- Private key written with default (world-readable) permissions at `src/charm.py:536-537`
- Branch name: `security/restrict-private-key-permissions`
- The fix: use `container.push()` with `permissions=0o600` for the private key file

<summary>Agent "Security fixes: zookeeper+pgbouncers" completed</summary>
<result>All three worktrees are set up correctly. Here is a summary of what was done:

**Finding 1: zookeeper-k8s-operator BUG-001** (Critical)
- Worktree: `/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/security-fixes/security-redact-passwords-from-config-log/`
- Branch: `security/redact-passwords-from-config-log`
- Modified: `src/managers/config.py` -- The `config_changed()` method at line 382-401 logged full property diffs (containing `ssl.keyStore.password`, `ssl.trustStore.password`) and full JAAS diffs (containing `user_<name>="<password>"`). The fix filters out sensitive property keys before logging and suppresses the JAAS diff entirely, replacing it with a simple "updating JAAS config" message.

**Finding 2: pgbouncer-k8s-operator BUG-004** (Critical)
- Worktree: `/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/security-fixes/security-use-env-for-monitoring-password/`
- Branch: `security/use-env-for-monitoring-password`
- Modified: `src/charm.py` -- The `_generate_monitoring_service()` method embedded the full connection string (with password) in the Pebble command. The fix moves it to a `PGB_EXPORTER_DSN` environment variable in the Pebble layer and references `$PGB_EXPORTER_DSN` in the command string.

**Finding 3: pgbouncer-operator BUG-003** (Critical)
- Worktree: `/home/ubuntu/charming-with-claude/experiments/2026-03-13-code-review-skills-our-data/charming-analysis/security-fixes/security-use-env-for-monitoring-password-vm/`
- Branch: `security/use-env-for-monitoring-password-vm`
- Modified: `src/charm.py` and `templates/prometheus-exporter.service.j2` -- The connection string was rendered directly into the systemd ExecStart line in a world-readable (0o644) service file. The fix writes the connection string to `/etc/pgbouncer-exporter.env` with 0o600 permissions, adds an `EnvironmentFile` directive to the systemd unit, and references `${PGBOUNCER_CONNECTION_STRING}` in ExecStart.

All three worktrees have the `fork` remote configured and a `COMMIT_MSG.txt` with a draft conventional commit message. Nothing has been committed or pushed.</result>
